I 3‑bet preflop to take the initiative, and this flop (Jh Js 2d) misses a large portion of Villain’s calling range (broadways, medium pairs, suited connectors). I hold A♠Q♠ — two overcards and a backdoor spade draw (and an Ace blocker) but currently only ace‑high, so my bet is mainly for fold equity and to charge any draws. A ~50% pot bet (1,000 into 2,000) is big enough to fold out many unpaired hands and deny free cards, yet small enough to keep the pot manageable if called. Plan: if he folds I take it down; if he calls I reassess on the turn and fire again on favorable cards (A/Q/spade) or pot‑control on blanks; if he check‑raises large I’ll likely fold this non‑paired hand. This line maximizes expected value by leveraging initiative, blockers and fold equity.

Pocket 5s is an underpair on a paired JJ2 rainbow flop, facing a bet from Minni after her BB 3-bet pre-flop. Her range heavily favors JJ (set), AJ (trips), overpairs like QQ/AA/KK, and broadway hands that connect well, crushing my hand with ~10-15% equity OOP. No draws, limited outs (3 fives, but many reverse), poor implied odds with ~40BB effective stacks behind and reverse implied risk if she has better. History shows Minni aggressive (frequent raises/calls), so her c-bet is wide but value-heavy here post-3bet. Check-fold is optimal to preserve ~68BB stack for superior spots in 3-handed tournament play, avoiding spew OOP with a bluff-catcher at best.

Folded to me on the button versus the big blind (Flash with 26k stack). Pot 150, to call 100 for a limp (BB posted). TcJd is a strong offsuit broadway hand with good playability, high equity versus calling range, and position. Standard play is to open-raise wide from the button to steal blinds or build pot OOP. Sizing to 300 (3x BB) is standard based on observed opening sizes in history (often 300). Postflop position advantage allows realization of equity. Folding too tight, limping poor strategy.

Facing a button open to 300 from the small blind with K♣8♥. This is a weak, offsuit hand that is easily dominated and plays poorly out of position — especially versus a button raiser who barrels frequently. The big blind (Flash) behind has a very deep stack and will often come along, creating multiway pots where K8o has minimal equity. With ~70BB effective and many better spots to pick, I should preserve chips and avoid difficult postflop decisions. Folding is the prudent play here.

Qd 2s is a very weak hand to defend from the Big Blind, even with the pot odds offered by the 300 raise. Out of position against a button open, this hand plays poorly post-flop and has significant reverse implied odds. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage.

Three-handed on the button with 8♠9♣ — a playable, positionally advantaged hand. I want to take the initiative, deny cheap multiway action, and leverage my position against both the SB (deep, calls a lot) and the BB (Elon, shows frequent 3x opens and postflop aggression). A standard 3x open to 300 gives good fold equity, keeps the pot manageable if I face a 3-bet, and leaves plenty of stack behind to play postflop. If I get isolated or called I can use position to realize equity; if I face a large 3-bet I can fold and preserve chips. Total to put in pot: 300.

T8o is a weak hand to defend from the small blind against a button open, especially being out of position for the rest of the hand. With a significant chip lead, there is no need to take marginal spots against the shorter stacks, as T8o often results in difficult post-flop decisions.

Preflop in the big blind facing a button raise to 300 (3x). My hand is A♦7♦, a suited ace with good playability, flush potential, and ace blocker against opponent's premium hands. Button range is wide in this short-handed game (3 players), especially given Minni's history of frequent raising from various positions, often with marginal hands (e.g., Hand 4, 13, 16). Pot odds are excellent: calling 200 into 450 (pot will be 650, ~3.25:1). Equity realization post-flop is strong OOP due to suited nature and nut flush potential. Folding is too tight; 3-betting risks too much with a non-premium hand against an aggressive opponent who may 4-bet or call wide. Calling keeps pot small, realizes equity well, and allows post-flop maneuvering.

Flopped top pair (sevens) with ace kicker, a very strong hand against Minni's wide button opening range, which includes many marginal hands based on history (frequent raises from button with speculative holdings). Board has 3d-6s-7c, featuring an open-ended straight draw (4-5) and backdoor flush draws, so betting denies equity to draws and worse made hands like smaller pairs or ace-high. OOP first to act, checking risks giving a free card or allowing cheap floats; betting builds pot for value from worse pairs (6x, weaker 7x), floats, and bluffs while protecting. Sizing ~60% pot (400 into 650) balances value extraction and fold equity against aggressive opponents without overcommitting early.

I have 8♠9♣ on a 3♦6♠7♣ flop — an open-ended straight draw (outs: four 5s + four 10s = 8 outs), roughly ~31.5% to hit by the river. Pot is 1,050 and Elon has bet 400; calling 400 gives ~27.6% pot odds (400 / (1,050+400)), so a call is +EV. I’m on the button so I can use position on the turn to apply pressure or realize equity; raising to the minimum (800) is a viable semi-bluff but unnecessarily bloats the pot and risks a shove or large reraise from an aggressive opponent. Folding is too tight given the pot odds. I’ll call to see the turn and play the draw in position.

Turn board 3♦6♠7♣5♣ completes straight draws (any 4x for 3-4-5-6-7), adds a club flush draw, making it scarier, but I hold top pair top kicker (7♦ with A♦ kicker), which blocks some 7x and provides nut flush blocker on diamonds. Minni's button calling range vs my flop bet (after her wide open) includes pairs below 7s (6x,5x), ace-high, stubborn floats, weaker two pairs, and some made straights or sets, but I'm ahead of much of it given history of calling stations (e.g., Hands 5,7,10,19). Pot 1450, OOP first to act: checking risks free cards to draws (clubs, runner-runner) or check-raises; betting extracts thin value from worse made hands/draws while denying equity. Sizing 1000 (~2/3 pot) balances fold equity vs aggressive lines and value from ~50% of calling range, leaving SPR low (~5:1 post-bet) for river control. Stacks deep enough (~6k), but early in tournament (hand 22/150), preserve while applying pressure on button defender.

I have 8♠9♣ on 3♦-6♠-7♣-5♣ — that’s a made 9-high straight on the turn (5-6-7-8-9). This is essentially the nut straight on this board; the only way to beat me is an identical 8+9 combo (a tie). Elon has been betting aggressively (c-bet 400 on the flop and now 1,000 on the turn into a 2,450 pot), so his range includes top pairs, two-pair/set candidates, and some bluffs. Most of those hands will pay to see more, so I want to extract value. Effective stacks are ~5,280 (Elon) and I have 6,320, so I can apply pressure but don’t need to shove. I’ll raise for value to a size that gets called by one-pair and two-pair hands but still leaves room for him to call or shove — raise to 3,000 total (adding 2,000). If he jams I’ll call (I have the nuts); if he calls I can re-evaluate the river and extract further value. Action: raise to 3,000 total.

Facing a turn raise to 3000 (additional 2000 into 5450 pot) after my continuation bet of 1000 on a board that completed obvious straights (3-4-5-6-7 with any 4x) and added a club flush draw. My A♦7♦ is top pair top kicker, but highly vulnerable: behind any 4x straight (A4s, K4s, suited connectors like 45, etc., all in her button calling range), sets (33/66), two pairs (65s, 35s, 67s), or stronger. Minni's history shows wide preflop opens and check-calls (e.g., Hands 5,7,10,19), but a turn raise after check-calling flop suggests strength or a semibluff draw (club flush draw with overcard/pair), but semibluffs less likely given sizing and board. Pot odds ~2.7:1 require ~27% equity, but my equity is crushed vs polarized raising range (~10-20% max, as TPTK rarely best). Calling commits ~40% stack OOP into low SPR (~2:1 post-call), facing river jam likely; raising all-in (min 5000 > her 3320 stack) gets no fold equity from value hands and poor vs bluffs. Early tournament (hand 22/150), stacks playable (5k+), folding preserves stack vs opponent's likely strong range while maintaining table image as disciplined (balanced prior lines). Best play: fold.

I am on the button with 8c 2h, which is an extremely weak hand. Although it is a 3-handed game, 82o has very little playability. Both opponents have shown aggressive tendencies in previous hands, so attempting a steal with such a low-equity hand is risky. I will fold and wait for a better opening.

Preflop in SB facing BB after Button folds. Hand is 84o, offsuit, disconnected low cards with poor playability. Standard play is to fold the SB rather than complete or raise, as it has low equity and difficult post-flop prospects against any defense. Preserving stack for better spots with 52BB effective depth.

Q2o is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against any calling range. On the button, position is favorable, but the pot odds (calling 100 into 150) do not justify entering with trash. Opponents have deep stacks and have shown aggression in prior hands. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this early tournament stage (hand 24/150). No value in raising or calling.

I’m in the small blind with A♥T♣ heads-up vs the big blind (button folded). This is a strong, high-equity hand in a blind-vs-blind spot — I want initiative and fold equity rather than just completing. A standard 3x BB sizing (300 total) puts pressure on Flash, extracts value from worse aces and broadways, and is small relative to my 8,770 stack (low risk). If Flash 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (often fold to very large 3-bets), but if he calls I’ll c-bet and play aggressively on A-high or dry boards and proceed cautiously on coordinated/flushy boards.

I am in the big blind with 9s 2h, which is an extremely weak hand. Minni raised from the small blind to 300. Although it only costs me 200 more to call, 92o is one of the worst starting hands in poker and plays poorly post-flop. Since I am currently the chip leader, there is no need to defend my blind with such a disadvantageous holding.

We're on the button with K♥3♦ in a 3-handed game. This is a weak but playable hand in position — we should be opening a wide range to steal the blinds and take advantage of positional postflop play. Pot is 150 and to call is 100; calling is too passive and folding is too tight. I will open-raise to 3x the BB (300) — large enough to apply pressure to the small blind and isolate the shorter-stacked big blind. If I get a large 3-bet I will fold K3o (dominated and poor kicker); if called I'll c-bet favorable flops and play in position. Raising to 300 maximizes fold equity and postflop options given the table dynamics and stack depths.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Nine offsuit. Minni, on the Button, has raised to 3BB (300). K9o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against an active opener like Minni. While I have a significant chip lead, there is no need to defend with a weak-medium hand and risk playing a bloated pot out of position. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

3-handed BB vs BTN raise to 3BB. QTo offsuit is a solid defending hand with decent equity, playability, and blockers against a wide button opening range (likely 40-50% of hands given Minni's aggressive history from button). Pot odds are favorable: calling 200 more into 450 (2.25:1 direct, better post-call). OOP but cheap, good implied odds with stacks (mine 51BB, Minni 86BB). Folding too tight; 3-betting risks too much with marginal hand. Call to realize equity and continue.

Flop gives me top pair with ten kicker (QH TH on 4s9sQd). Decent but vulnerable hand OOP vs aggressive BTN raiser with wide range (40-50% including overpairs like JJ+, AKo, suited broadways, connectors, flush/straight draws). Two spades on board heighten draw factor. Pot 650 (~6.5BB), deep effective stacks (mine 49BB, her 86BB). Leading OOP with TPK non-standard; check to realize equity, control pot, induce cbets from ~60-70% of her range (worse pairs/draws/bluffs), and retain fold equity post-turn. Plan: Check-call small/medium bets (~400-600), fold to large bets or check-behind if checked to. Folding too tight pre-turn; raising risks vs strong range. Prioritizes stack preservation long-term tournament while exploiting aggression.

We opened K3o preflop to use position and wide stealing range. Flop comes 4♠ 9♠ Q♦ — I missed but I’m on the button in position and Elon checked. This is a great spot for a continuation bet to take the pot down and apply pressure to his wide defending range (many offsuit cards, weaker broadways, and non-paired hands). The board has two spades so some of his calls will be draws, but a ~1/2 pot stab preserves stack size while giving good fold equity. Plan: c-bet a medium size and fold to a large check-raise; if called proceed cautiously on turn and re-evaluate. Bet size chosen: 325 (≈½ pot).

Facing a half-pot cbet (325 into 650 pre-bet pot, now 975 total) on a drawy flop (Qd9s4s, spade flush draw, gutshots like JT, 87). Holding top pair weak kicker (QQ with T) OOP vs button's wide range (40-50% hands: pairs, broadways, suited connectors, Ax). Equity ~45-55% vs polarized cbet range (value: QQ+, AK, sets; bluffs/draws: flush draws, straight draws, lower pairs). Excellent pot odds (325 to win 975, ~3:1, need 25%). Deep stacks (49BB eff) support calling to realize equity, control pot size, and induce from worse (KJ/QJ worse Qx, draws). Raising OOP bloats pot, vulnerable to 3bets from AA/KK/sets; folding too tight given strength and history of Minni's aggression (frequent cbets). Plan: call, evaluate turn (fold to strong aggression if improves draws, bet/fold if bricks). Prioritizes long-term stack growth by exploiting wide ranges.

Turn Kc bricks most flush draws but improves key parts of Minni's wide button range: Kx (AK, KQ, KJ, suited connectors like KTs/JTs) to top pair; completes JT straight draws (common in 40-50% button opens). My QhTd is now second-top pair (Queens) with weak Ten kicker on paired-pair potential board (Qx vulnerable to any Kx or better Q). Equity drops to ~35-45% vs her polarized c-bet range (value: KK+, QJ+, sets/trips; bluffs/draws: missed spades, gutshots, floats). Pot 1300 (~13BB), effective stacks deep (46BB/83BB). OOP first to act: Betting risks bloating pot with marginal hand vs 3-bet range, poor EV (fold equity low given history of check-calls); folding irrelevant. Check standard for pot control, realize equity cheaply, induce cbets from ~50-60% weaker hands (worse Qx like QJ/Q9, missed draws like AhXh? spades, 87/56), exploit her aggression (frequent multi-street barreling observed in hands 2,6,15,18,22). Plan: Check-call small/medium (~400-800, pot odds >3:1), fold to large/overbets; check-behind possible if she checks. Preserves stack for better spots while defending wide pre/flop spots profitably long-term.

I have top pair (K) with a weak 3 kicker on 4♠-9♠-Q♦-K♣. Elon called my preflop raise and my flop c-bet, then checked the turn — his line is consistent with Qx, 9x, medium pairs, or a drawing/float type. Betting is correct here to get value from worse pairs and to protect against backdoor equity. The pot is 1,300, so I size ~2/3 pot to 900: large enough to extract value from Qx/9x and charge turn/river improvers, but not so large that it needlessly commits my stack. If Elon check-raises big (pot-sized or shove) I’ll give credit to a very strong holding and fold; I’ll call smaller raises and re-evaluate on the river if called.

Facing a 900 bet into 2200 pot (~40% pot) on turn Kc after check-check preflop (defended wide BB vs BTN), check-call flop cbet with top pair weak kicker on drawy board (4s9sQd). Kc bricks spades but massively improves villain's wide button range (40-50% hands: AK/KQ/KJ/KTs top pair now, JT straights, overpairs QQ+/AA, sets). My QhTd is now 2nd pair (Queens & Tens), OOP, with poor kicker vs likely Kx/value. Equity ~25-35% vs polarized turn betting range (value heavy post-K: KK+, AK, KQ, sets/trips; some bluffs like missed Ax spades, gutshots but less as board improves value). Pot odds require ~29% (900/3100), borderline but poor implied odds OOP (river aggression from Kx/draws completes beat us; we cap range). Raising commits too much (~20% stack) with marginal hand vs 3bet jam range (KK+/sets). Calling builds pot OOP into river where we fold to further aggression (>60% likely vs value). Minni's aggressive history (cbets multi-streets: hands 2,6,15,16,18,22) but turn bet sizing/value-oriented after flop cbet. Folding preserves 46BB stack for better spots (150-hand tourney, ICM irrelevant early), exploits wide ranges profitably long-term by not over-defending. Plan post-fold: tighten BB defends slightly vs her BTN, 3bet light IP.

I am holding Seven of Diamonds and Deuce of Diamonds on the button. While these are suited, 7-2 is the mathematically weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Despite having a significant chip lead and being in position, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot with such a weak hand, especially since the blinds have shown willingness to defend or 3-bet in previous hands. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

K4o is a marginal hand from the small blind facing only the big blind after button fold. While pot odds of calling 50 into 150 (3:1) suggest needing ~20% equity, and K4o likely realizes around 35% against a typical BB defending range, the hand has poor playability postflop: offsuit, disconnected, and easily dominated by better kings or pairs. Minni has shown aggression postflop in history, making thin value bets likely. Raising is not justified with this weak hand. Folding preserves the stack for better opportunities early in the tournament with 45BB effective stack.

Preflop on the button with 2h8s, an offsuit, unconnected, low-card hand with very poor playability and showdown value. Facing a likely small blind raise to 200 (to call 100 into 150 pot), this is a standard fold even from position. Opponents have large stacks, but no need to speculate with trash; preserve stack for better spots in this tournament phase.

I have 4c3d in the small blind — a very weak, offsuit, low connector. With Elon folded it’s effectively SB vs BB. The price to see a flop is tiny (call 50 more), so I’ll defend and keep the pot small rather than folding away the blind or inflating the pot OOP with a marginal hand. Raising to 200 would invite wider defense or 3-bets from Flash and commit chips with little postflop playability. Plan: call (total 100 committed), see the flop, and fold to significant aggression unless I hit a strong pair/draw or can take the pot with a controlled line.

I have Jack-Ten suited, which is a very strong starting hand in a short-handed game. Minni limped from the small blind, which often indicates a capped or weak range. By raising here, I take the initiative and build a pot with a hand that has great post-flop playability and equity. A standard raise to 350 total is appropriate to put pressure on the limper.

I’m in the small blind with 4♣3♦ — a very weak, offsuit, low connector. Flash (BB) has raised to 350 after I’d posted 50, so I need to add 250 more (committing 350 total). The pot is 450 now; calling 250 gets me a ~35.7% break-even equity (pot will be ~700). Versus a wide BB opening range, defending cheaply is reasonable — folding surrenders the blind too easily and a raise to the 600 minimum is unnecessary and too large out of position with this holding. Plan: call 350 total, see a flop, and fold to significant aggression unless I hit a real pair or a strong draw. If I get a favorable, low-commitment flop, I can continue; otherwise I’ll protect my stack and let it go.

Board: 7♣ 10♥ 7♦. I hold 4♣3♦ — complete air. I missed entirely: no pair and only a very remote backdoor straight (would need both a 5 and a 6). The flop is paired, which increases the likelihood an opponent already has a 7 or will improve to trips/full house; the Ten is also a common card in preflop raising ranges. Flash opened preflop and is capable of having strong hands and applying pressure; betting out OOP into two opponents with zero showdown value is an easy way to bloat the pot and get exploited. Pot control and stack preservation are priority — check and fold to any meaningful bet. If action checks through I’ll take a free turn card and re-evaluate, but I will not barrel this spot as a bluff given the two opponents and paired board.

I have top pair (Tens) with a Jack kicker on a paired board (7-10-7). Minni checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise. This board texture is generally good for the pre-flop aggressor. I will bet for value, targeting weaker Tens, mid-pocket pairs, or Ace-high hands that might call. A bet of 350 (half pot) is standard to build the pot and protect my equity against overcards.

I hold 4♣3♦ in the small blind, heads-up vs Flash after Elon folded. Flop is 7♣ 10♥ 7♦; I checked and Flash bets 350 into a pot of 1050, so calling 350 would win a 1400 pot (break-even equity = 350/1400 = 25%). With 3-4 offsuit I have essentially no equity (only a runner-runner straight possibility), and the paired board makes it much more likely Flash has a 7, a 10, trips, or another strong holding. Flash opened preflop and continues with a sizable flop bet, a line that is weighted toward value in a heads-up pot. I’m out of position, have no realistic draw or fold equity, and a bluff/raise here is too risky and unlikely to work. For stack preservation and long-term EV, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I’m on the button with A♣8♣ — a strong, positionally advantaged hand with suit. It costs 100 to see the flop in a 150 pot, so raising is the standard play to seize initiative, pick up the blinds, and build pots in position. I’ll open to 300 (a routine ~3x sizing): it applies pressure to the 44bb big blind (Elon) and the deep small blind (Flash), preserves postflop maneuverability for my 9,295 stack, and gives me good playability if called. If I face a significant 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and opponent; otherwise I expect to play postflop in position with a suited Ace and good fold equity.

I am in the Small Blind with 6-3 offsuit, which is an extremely weak hand. Minni has raised from the Button, and with a junk hand like this, there is no reason to defend or contest the pot out of position against the dealer's range. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

3-handed, facing a button raise to 300 after SB fold. Pot 450, calling 200 more (pot odds ~2.25:1, requiring ~31% equity). Jd7o is a marginal but defensible hand in BB vs wide BTN range (likely 45-55% of hands). Equity around 36-40% vs typical BTN open range. OOP but good implied odds with SPR ~7 post-call. Folding too tight in short-handed play; raising not justified with offsuit non-connected hand. Calling to realize equity and play post-flop.

Flop board Qh9c8h is draw-heavy with possible straights, two-tone hearts. Holding Jd7c for jack-high, gutshot straight draw (4 outs to 10 for Q-J-T-9-8), and backdoor club flush. Equity realization OOP vs BTN wide c-betting range (high frequency ~60-70% here). SPR ~6.5 postflop supports calling small cbets but check first to pot control, induce bluffs/checks, or get correct odds. Leading risks fold equity loss or getting raised off equity; not ideal with non-nutted draw and no strong blockers. Historical play shows Minni cbets flops often after opens. Check to play optimally postflop.

Flop is 9♣ 8♥ Q♥ and I hold A♣8♣ — middle pair with the Ace kicker and two clubs (backdoor nut potential). Only Elon (BB) is left and I have position. This is a good spot to c‑bet for value and protection: I want to charge worse pairs, A‑x and some Qx turned into bluff-catchers, and deny free cards to heart/club runner-runner draws. I’ll size to about half the pot to get value while keeping a shove from his ~42BB stack from forcing an immediate, extremely marginal decision. Plan: bet ~½ pot (325). If he raises/shoves I’ll re-evaluate (a large shove likely indicates a polarized/strong range and I’ll fold; I’ll call smaller raises more often), and if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on a Queen or heart turn.

Facing a 325 c-bet into 650 pot (now 975, odds 3:1 requiring ~25% equity) on Qh9c8h board, which is coordinated with flush/straight draws favoring villain's button range but also my gutshot straight draw (4 outs to T for QJT98) and backdoor club flush draw. Jd7c blocks some JT draws and has decent equity (~30-35% vs Minni's likely wide c-bet range including bluffs/medium pairs). OOP but low SPR post-call (~3) allows controlled pot; folding too tight in 3-handed vs button aggression where Minni frequently cbets flops. Raising risks getting pushed on with marginal draw; calling realizes equity, induces turns, and leverages history of Minni's frequent continuation betting. Pot odds excellent, implied decent with stacks.

Turn board Qh9c8hAh is extremely coordinated and dangerous, completing nut flush draws (hearts) and providing strong straight outs (e.g., T for QJT98). Holding Jd7c for jack-high and a gutshot straight draw to the T (4 outs, ~8% equity to improve), but no flush draw, backdoor nothing relevant now, and poor showdown value OOP vs Minni's button range. Pre-flop call and flop call defended marginally with gutshot but now equity crushed as Ah smashes villain's continuing range (hearts heavy like KhJh, AhX, suited connectors). Pot 1300, SPR ~3 with my 3930 stack supports controlled play, but leading bets out air/draw and invites raises/shoves from value; no fold equity. Checking optimal: induces bluffs/checks from missed draws/medium pairs (Minni cbets flops frequently per history but may slow down on scary turn), realizes equity passively, and preserves stack for better spots in 3-handed dynamic. Will fold to most bets lacking pot odds (~25%+ needed vs typical sizing); check-behind possible if weak. Maximizes EV long-term over 150 hands by avoiding spew.

I have A♣8♣ on 9♣ 8♥ Q♥ A♥ — two pair (A8) in position against a single caller who passively called my flop bet and checked the turn. That line suggests a lot of medium-strength hands (Qx, 9x, Kx, small pairs, some Ax) rather than an already-polarized value shove (made flushes/straight/sets). The board is dangerous — three hearts and JT-type straight combos — so I want to charge and protect now rather than give a free river. I will bet for value and protection about 60% of the pot to get called by worse pairs and charge drawing hands while keeping the pot controllable against a shove. If Elon makes a small raise I will call and re-evaluate on the river; if he jams hard (polarized shove) I will be cautious — his range is likely to include hands that beat me (flushes, AQ/A9, sets) and I may fold to an obvious polarized all-in depending on sizing and read.

Turn Ah completes the nut flush draw on a highly coordinated board (Qh9c8hAh), smashing villain's button range which favors hearts (suited connectors, broadway hearts like KhJh, AhX). Jd7c holds only a weak gutshot straight draw to T (4 outs, ~8% equity to improve) with no pair, flush draw, or relevant blockers, and zero showdown value OOP vs Minni's frequent c-betting style now turning into likely value on scary card. Pot 2100, facing 800 bet requires ~28% equity to call (800/(2100+800+3930 post-call consideration)), but realized equity <20% vs her continuing range (value heavy post-flush card). SPR post-call ~1 commits stack on river regardless, but without sufficient equity or fold equity via raise (raises get shoved on), folding maximizes EV by avoiding spew in 3-handed dynamic over 150 hands. History shows Minni cbets flops wide but turns bets here indicate strength; preserve 3930 stack for premium spots.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with a speculative hand (76o). While 76o is at the bottom of a standard opening range, the wide dynamics of 3-handed play and my chip lead allow for a steal attempt. Elon in the small blind is relatively short-stacked (under 40BB), so I must be prepared to fold if he shoves. Minni in the big blind is active, but I will have positional advantage post-flop if she calls. A standard 3x open is appropriate here.

7h9d is an offsuit gapped hand with poor playability out of position in the small blind. Facing a button raise from an aggressive player (Flash), with BB (Minni) behind who often defends. Pot odds are 450:250 (~1.8:1), but required equity against button's wide range is too high considering multiway potential and OOP disadvantage post-flop. Stack depth (38BB) allows waiting for better spots. Folding preserves stack for premium opportunities.

Heads-up vs Button who opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with 4♦6♦ — a low suited connector with decent postflop playability (flush/straight potential) and good implied odds heads-up. The open is only ~3x; calling 200 into a 450 pot gives ~30.8% immediate pot odds, and this hand usually has >30% equity vs a typical button opening range. I don’t want to 3-bet OOP with a speculative hand and inflate the pot or face a 4-bet. I’ll defend and see a flop, playing cautiously depending on texture.

I’m first to act out of position on a paired board (Td Ts Qd) holding 4♦6♦. I have no pair but I do have a four-card diamond draw (9 outs) and ~34–35% to make a flush by the river, plus very little showdown value otherwise. Pot is 650 and Flash opened wide preflop, so he will c-bet this texture frequently. I don’t want to lead OOP with a naked draw and inflate the pot or face a check-raise; checking keeps pot control and lets me call reasonable c-bets where my equity and implied odds make the call profitable. If he checks back I can take a free card or fire the turn on a favorable card. Therefore I’ll check and see his reaction.

The flop is Td-Ts-Qd, which is a very coordinated and paired board. I have 7-high with only the 7 of diamonds as a very weak backdoor flush draw. Minni, defending from the Big Blind, has a range that contains many Tens, Queens, and straight draws (KJ, J9). Since this board hits her defending range quite hard and I have zero showdown value or significant equity, a continuation bet is unlikely to succeed often enough to be profitable. Checking back allows me to see a free turn card and potentially realize what little equity I have or evaluate if I can represent a hand on a later street if she continues to check.

I have 4♦6♦ on Td Ts Qd A♥. I only have a diamond flush draw (9 outs) with one card to come — ~9/46 ≈ 19.6% to improve on the river. Pot is 650 and my stack is 9,670. I have essentially no showdown value now and am out of position. Betting as a semi-bluff would require substantial fold equity to be +EV; given Flash opened wide preflop and checked the flop, his calling range likely contains many hands (T/Q/A/high-card) that will call a turn probe or continue on the river, so my fold equity is limited. I prefer pot control and preserving my stack for better spots: check and see the river for a free card (or induce a river bluff), then decide based on the runout and any bet size. Therefore I will check.

The turn brings the Ace of Hearts, which is a significant scare card. However, this board (Td-Ts-Qd-Ah) is extremely coordinated and likely connects with many hands in Minni's big blind defending range, such as Jacks, Kings, or even slow-played Tens and Queens. Having checked the flop, my hand (7-high) has zero showdown value. While the Ace is a good card for a pre-flop raiser to represent, Minni's second check doesn't necessarily mean she's weak; she could be trapping or planning to check-call with a marginal made hand or a straight draw (like KJ or J9). Given the board texture and my lack of equity, I will check back to see a free river and avoid inflating the pot with a total air hand.

River: T♦ T♠ Q♦ A♥ 9♠. I hold 4♦6♦ — missed my flush and have no pair or showdown value. Pot is 650, stacks are deep (my stack 9,670, Villain 25,800) and both players checked flop and turn, so Villain’s range contains many hands that beat me (T/Q/A, pocket pairs) and will call a river bluff. Fold equity is thin and a bluff here is low EV; I preserve stack and control risk. I check to reach showdown; if Villain then fires a meaningful bet I’ll fold this river with no pair and no blocker to relevant two-pair/trips hands.

I have 7-high on a very coordinated board (Td-Ts-Qd-Ah-9s) and absolutely no showdown value. Minni has checked for the third time, indicating she likely has a weak hand or a marginal made hand she wants to take to showdown for free. While my hand loses to almost any two cards in her range (even her 'air' hands like K5 or J4 are higher than my 7), the pot is small (650) and my previous checks have capped my range, making a bluff less believable. In a 3-handed tournament where I have a significant chip lead, there is no reason to risk chips on a low-frequency bluff. I will check and concede the pot.

3-handed on the button with As9o facing an SB limp (Minni completed the SB) and presumed BB check (Flash). Pot is 150, to call 100. A9o is a strong isolating hand here: ace-high with decent kicker, good blocker to AA/AK/AX, and excellent post-flop playability in position. Opponents' stacks are deep (Minni 99BB, Flash 260BB), but my 39BB stack allows for aggression. Standard iso-raise size around 3x the BB vs limp. Raising to 300 builds the pot, takes initiative, and folds out weaker hands while getting value from worse aces/pairs. Folding or limping is too passive in this spot.

I have Jc9s in the SB facing a BTN open to 300 (pot 450, 250 to call). J9o is playable in 3-handed but I'm out of position post-flop to both opponents and Flash (BB) is very deep — raising here risks a shove or a big 3‑bet and bloating the pot OOP with a marginal hand. Calling keeps the pot controllable, lets me realize equity vs a wide BTN opening range, and preserves stack depth to exploit favorable flops. Pot odds are reasonable (250 to call into a 700 pot ≈ 2.8:1). Plan post-flop: continue with top pair or strong draws, fold to heavy aggression on dry boards; avoid unnecessary isolation. Action: call (total committed 300).

I am in the big blind with 8c 4s. Elon (the button) raised to 300, and Minni (the small blind) called. I have to put in 200 more to call into a pot that would be 900. While I am getting decent pot odds (4.5:1), 8-4 offsuit is an extremely weak hand. Out of position against two opponents, one of whom (Elon) is playing very short-stacked (only ~35BB left), this hand has very poor playability and will often be dominated or put me in difficult spots post-flop. I've been playing relatively conservatively and there's no need to defend such a weak range here.

I called preflop with Jc9s to keep the pot small and realize equity. Flop Qh 7s 4d leaves me with no pair and no realistic draw (only J-high, no meaningful flush/straight outs). Elon opened from the button so his c-bet range is heavy with Qx, overpairs and broadways that dominate me. Out of position and with healthy stacks, I want pot control — check and fold to meaningful aggression. If he checks back I can see a cheap turn; if he leads a small probe (<~30% pot) I may consider a call to see a turn, but I’ll fold to standard/large c-bets (~1/2 pot or bigger).

Preflop, I raised with A9o on the button after Minni limped from SB, which is standard for initiative and range advantage. Flop is Qh7s4d, a dry rainbow board that misses most of Minni's wide SB calling range (suited connectors, weak Ax, small pairs). My preflop raising range hits this board decently with QQ/77/44/AQ/AK/overpairs. Minni checks, giving me a free opportunity to continuation bet for value against worse Ax (AJ-AT, KQ), thin value from overcards/draws, and high fold equity against air/missed draws. Ace-high has decent showdown value if checked through, but betting builds the pot when ahead and denies equity. With ~36BB effective stack (shorter than opponents), I apply pressure postflop. Sizing 50-60% pot (350-450) is balanced to get calls from worse while protecting against floats. Betting 400 extracts value/maximizes EV.

I have Jc9s on a dry Qh-7s-4d flop and I'm out of position to Elon's button opening range. Pot is 1,100 and Elon led for 400 (I must call 400). I have no pair and no realistic draw (only remote backdoors). Although the raw pot-odds to call are cheap (need ~400/(1,100+400+400)=~21% equity to break even), Elon's range from the button will include a lot of Qx, mid/high broadways and pocket pairs that dominate me. This is a marginal hand OOP on a dry board where continuing only to realize equity invites difficult turn decisions and potential pressure given his ~31 BB stack. My preflop plan was pot control and folding to meaningful aggression; this bet is borderline and I prefer to conserve chips and wait for a better spot. Action: fold.

Button in a 3-handed pot with 4♣8♣ offsuit is marginal but playable — position is my biggest advantage. No prior raise, so I want to take the initiative and steal the blinds or play a capped pot in position. I’ll open to a standard 3xBB (300) — big enough to apply pressure on the small blind and discourage a light defend from the shorter big blind (Elon ~41BB), but not so large that I overcommit with a weak hand. If called I’ll proceed cautiously post‑flop (small c-bets on dry flops, fold to heavy resistance). If faced with a sizable 3-bet or an all-in preflop, I’ll fold this holding.

I am holding QTs in the Small Blind, which is a strong suited broadway hand, especially in a 3-handed game. Minni has raised from the Button, a position where players typically open with a wide range of hands. Although I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, QTs has excellent playability and equity. Calling the 250 additional chips provides me with good pot odds to see a flop. I prefer calling over 3-betting here to keep the pot controlled and avoid a potential 4-bet shove from Minni or Elon, who is relatively short-stacked in the Big Blind.

65o is a weak offsuit hand with limited playability, especially out of position in a multiway pot after a button raise and small blind call. Pot odds are about 3.5:1 (200 to call into 700), but my equity against their likely ranges is insufficient, particularly post-flop OOP where I'll struggle to realize equity. Folding preserves my 42BB stack for better spots in this tournament.

On this flop of Kd-Th-6s, I have flopped middle pair with a Queen kicker. While a King is certainly in Minni's button-opening range, I have a solid hand that can call a continuation bet. Out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. If Minni bets, I will most likely call to see a turn card, as I have middle pair and potential to improve to two pair or a straight (with a Jack).

Flop: K♦ T♥ 6♠ — I raised preflop with 4♥8♣ and have no pair and virtually no meaningful draw (only a very remote two-card straight). Flash called my 3x open from the SB, so his continuing range contains a lot of Kx, Tx, broadways and pairs that dominate me. Given his tendency to call and my lack of equity, a c-bet will often be called and I’ll be forced to fold to any real resistance. I’ll check to control the pot size, realize my (small) equity in position, and fold to meaningful pressure; if he checks back I get a free turn to improve or continue as a bluff on a favorable card.

On the turn (8d), the board is Kd-Th-6s-8d. I have middle pair (Tens) with a Queen kicker. After both players checked the flop, it's possible that Minni (the pre-flop aggressor) doesn't have a King. However, my hand has solid showdown value but is not strong enough to value bet for three streets. Checking again is a prudent play to control the pot and see what Minni does. If she bets, I have an easy call given the flop action, and if she checks, I likely have the best hand going into the river.

I opened preflop, checked the dry K‑T‑6 flop to control the pot and realized equity — the turn 8♦ pairs my 8 and gives me a small (second) pair in position. Flash (SB) called preflop and has checked both streets, so his continuing range contains a lot of Kx/TX, some medium pairs and a fair amount of floats/weak broadways. A half‑pot bet here (350 into 700) is a cheap way to extract value from worse hands (weaker pairs, some 7x/9x/6x, floats) and deny a free river to cards that could improve to beat me, while keeping the pot manageable if I’m facing resistance. If he check‑raises or shows heavy aggression I’m folding — my hand is not strong enough to call big raises; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river and proceed cautiously.

On the turn, the 8d doesn't significantly change the board texture. After both players checked the flop, Minni's 350 bet into a 700 pot suggests she's either trying to take the pot down after a missed continuation bet or value betting a marginal hand. With middle pair (Tens) and a decent kicker (Queen), I have enough equity to call, especially given the 3-to-1 pot odds. I am not strong enough to raise, but folding would be too tight given the action so far.

On the river, the board is Kd-Th-6s-8d-Jh. I still hold a pair of Tens. The Jack is an overcard to my pair and completes potential straights like Q9. Since both players checked the flop and Minni bet the turn, her range is somewhat wide but could include a Ten, an Eight, or a straight draw. By checking, I allow Minni to check behind with marginal hands that I might beat, or I can evaluate a bet if she decides to fire again. Betting myself doesn't accomplish much as I'm unlikely to be called by many worse hands.

Board K♦ T♥ 6♠ 8♦ J♥ — I hold 4♥8♣, so I have second pair (eights) on the river. Line: I opened preflop, checked the dry K‑T‑6 flop, then bet a small half‑pot (350) on the turn and Flash (SB) called. That call suggests a range heavy in Kx/Tx/medium pairs and some floats — many of which beat my pair (Kx, Tx, 6x, sets, Jx that pairs on the river, and straights like Q9/97). A river bet is thin: worse hands that I beat will often fold, while a lot of hands that beat me will call. Betting also risks a raise that I’m not comfortable calling. With 90+BB effective and no strong read that the SB will fold better hands, the lowest‑variance, most +EV line is to check and take my hand to showdown. I’ll check and accept showdown rather than fire a thin value bet or get turned away by a raise.

I am on the button with pocket sixes (6h 6d). This is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. I have the chip lead, while Elon is relatively short-stacked (41 BBs) and Minni has a moderate stack (89 BBs). A standard 3x open is appropriate here to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds. If either opponent shoves, I will have to evaluate the odds, but 66 is a solid hand to play aggressively on the button.

Preflop as SB facing a standard 3x open from the Button (Flash, deep-stacked and aggressive from history). My holding 9d3d is suited but low, gapped, and weak overall. While SB defends wider due to odds (pot 450, call 250 gives ~2.8:1), 93s has poor playability OOP, limited strong draw potential, and low showdown value. Equity vs wide BTN range is marginal (~25-30%), but postflop position is awful. BB yet to act could squeeze. Optimal to fold weak hands like this and preserve stack (41BB) for premium spots to maximize long-term EV over 150 hands.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I’m BB with 9♠8♥ and ~89BB effective. Calling the extra 200 (300 total in pot from me) is the right play: 9-8o has decent equity vs a wide button opening range in 3-handed play and the pot odds (~30.8% break-even) plus deep stacks give good implied odds to realize straights and two-pair. 3-betting OOP with a marginal hand is suboptimal and folding is too tight given the price. I’ll call, see the flop, and proceed based on texture (continue with draws/second pairs, or fold to heavy aggression on dry high-card boards).

Check. I have 9♠8♥ on a Q♦ 2♥ 5♥ board: no pair, only a weak backdoor heart possibility (need runner‑runner hearts) and very little immediate equity. The button opened wide preflop and will c‑bet often, but with deep effective stacks (~87–89BB) I don't want to build a pot out of position with a marginal hand. Checking controls the pot and lets me see a turn for free. Plan: if Flash checks back I take a free turn and can proceed only when I improve. If Flash continuation‑bets small (≈≤½ pot) I’ll call to realize equity and pursue implied odds to hit a pair or straight/heart-runner; I’ll fold to large sizing or any check‑raise. If I pair or pick up significant equity on the turn I’ll start betting; otherwise I preserve my stack. Action: check (0).

I hold pocket sixes on a flop of 2h-Qd-5h. While the Queen is an overcard, my hand is likely ahead of much of Minni's defending range from the big blind (suited connectors, smaller pairs, or air). Minni has checked to me. I will place a continuation bet of approximately half the pot to deny equity to her overcards and draws, and to see where I stand in the hand. If she calls, I can re-evaluate on the turn, likely looking for a cheap showdown.

Flash c‑bet 300 into a 950 pot. Calling 300 gives me 300 to win 1250 (break‑even ≈24%). With 9♠8♥ on Q♦2♥5♥ I have no pair but a backdoor heart and very weak straight backdoors; versus a wide 3‑handed BTN c‑bet range my equity is roughly in the mid‑20s%, so calling is +EV given the small sizing and deep effective stacks (~87–89BB) which provide good implied odds. I don’t want to build the pot OOP with a marginal hand, so I’ll call to see a turn and re‑evaluate — fold to strong raise or heavy double‑barrel aggression, call small turn bets, and start betting only if I pair or pick up meaningful equity.

Check. After calling the flop I have 9♠8♥ and the turn 7♣ removes the runner‑runner heart possibility — I now only have six outs (three 9s + three 8s) → ~6/46 ≈ 13% to pair on the river. Flash checked the turn, which shows some weakness, but leading OOP with almost no equity against a wide calling range and huge effective stacks is high variance and likely unprofitable. Checking controls the pot (1250) and allows me to realize my ~13% equity for free; if I pair the river I can extract value or raise, if I miss I can fold to sizable aggression. Plan: see the river for free; call a small river probe when the odds justify it, fold to large river pressure unless I improve.

I hold pocket sixes on a 2h-Qd-5h-7c board. I bet the flop and was called by Minni, which suggests she either has a piece of the board (likely a Queen or a smaller pair) or a draw (likely hearts). The 7c on the turn is relatively blank. Minni has checked again. While I could bet to protect against draws, checking back here is a safer line to control the pot size and reach showdown. If I bet and get raised, I'll be in a difficult spot. Checking back allows me to see the river for free and potentially catch bluffs or win against missed draws or smaller pairs.

Board is 2♥ Q♦ 5♥ 7♣ 8♠; I hold 9♠8♥ — one pair, eights. Pot is 1,250 and it’s checked to me on the river. Villain opened BTN preflop, c‑bet small on the flop (300) and then checked turn and river. That line is most consistent with a wide opening range that missed the turn/river (air/overcards) or pot‑control with a Qx; big hands (sets / two pair) are less likely given the passive turn/river. My pair of eights has decent showdown value against bluffs/missed hands and lower pairs (77, 55, 22) but loses to Qx and any 9x or better. Betting here would only get called by hands that beat me (Qx) or possibly some worse pairs; it has limited value and risks bloating the pot OOP with a marginal hand. With no bet to call and deep stacks, the lowest‑variance, +EV line is to check and take my showdown. Action: check (0).

On the river, the board is 2h-Qd-5h-7c-8s. I have pocket sixes, which is a marginal showdown hand. Minni has checked for the third time this hand. Given the action, she likely has a weak Queen, a smaller pair that beats mine (like 7x or 8x which improved on the turn/river), or a missed heart draw. If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I might get check-raised by a trapped monster. Checking back is the safest way to realize my equity and reach showdown without risking more chips against a range that could easily contain a better pair.

3-handed on the button with pocket threes (33), a solid small pair with good equity against wide calling ranges. Standard play is to raise for value, isolation, and position. Opponents frequently open to 300 in similar spots from history. Raising to 300 (3x BB) builds the pot while maintaining fold equity against blinds. Limp or fold would be suboptimal given position and hand strength. My stack of 4130 (41BB) supports this without overcommitting.

Elon opened to 300 from the button, pot is 450 and I have T9o in the small blind. This is a 3-handed table so opening ranges are wide; T9o has decent equity and good post‑flop playability against a wide button raising range. Flash (BB) is deep and often defends, so a small 3‑bet or min‑raise would likely either bloat the pot OOP or get called by worse hands — I don’t want to build a big pot out of position with a marginal hand. Calling keeps the pot controllable, preserves stack depth for post‑flop play, and gives me the chance to realize equity or fold to heavy action. I will call the raise (put a total of 300 into the pot).

I am in the Big Blind with King-Nine offsuit. Elon (Button) opened for 300, and Minni (Small Blind) called. The pot is currently 700 and it costs me 200 more to call. I am getting 3.5-to-1 odds on a call, and King-Nine is strong enough to defend in a multi-way pot from the big blind, especially given my significant stack lead over Elon. Closing the action and seeing a flop with a king-high hand in this spot is a standard profitable play.

Flop texture: A♦ 6♣ 3♣. My hand T♠9♣ has no pair and only a backdoor club runner-runner draw (one club in hand + two on flop = backdoor). In a 3-handed pot the button opened wide and will have a lot of Ax and broadway combos that connect with this Ace; the big blind is very deep and tends to defend/call a lot. I'm out of position to both. Betting as a bluff here is low EV — I will often be called by better hands or draws and I don't want to bloat a pot OOP with essentially zero showdown equity. Check to control the pot, realize equity, and fold to meaningful aggression. If both opponents check through I can evaluate the turn and consider a delayed c-bet only if I pick up equity or get a clearly favorable card; if faced with a sizeable bet I will fold (only consider floating very small probes). For now: check.

I have King-high on an Ace-high board with a flush draw. I completely missed the flop. Minni has checked to me. There is no reason to bet here as I have very little equity and am unlikely to fold out better hands. I will check and see what the pre-flop aggressor (Elon) does.

Flop is 6c 3c Ad, giving me top set with pocket 3s (3s 3d pairing the 3c on board). This is an extremely strong hand, likely the nuts against most ranges, beating any ace, two-pair, or draws. Both opponents checked, indicating weakness, but there's a flush draw (two clubs) and ace blocker that could induce action from Ax or draws. Pot is 900, my stack 3830 (over 4x pot). Betting 2/3 pot (600) extracts value from weaker Ax, pairs, and draws while building the pot with position. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or allowing bluffs. Standard value bet sizing here balances protection and value.

I checked the flop as planned; Elon C-bets 600 into a 900 pot (pot now 1500). Calling 600 requires ~29% equity (600/(1500+600)=28.6%). On A♦6♣3♣ with T♠9♣ I have no pair and only a runner-runner club possibility — effectively negligible equity vs a reasonable c-bet range that contains many Ax, pairs and strong broadway combos. This is multiway and I’m out of position to both players (Flash is deep and tends to defend), so floating or bluff‑raising OOP here is low EV. A raise to 1200 would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and will often be called or re-raised by better hands. With healthy stack depth and many hands left in the tournament, the correct play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I have King-high on an Ace-high board. Elon, the pre-flop aggressor, has continued with a bet of 600 into a 1500 pot. Minni has already folded. My hand has no showdown value and no draws. Continuing in this spot against the original raiser on this board texture with my specific hand is unprofitable. I will fold.

I have pocket 4s on the button in a 3-handed game — a hand worth playing for value and for set-mining with the positional advantage. I want to take the initiative, apply fold equity to the blinds, and avoid playing a multiway pot if possible, so I’ll open-raise. A standard 3x BB to 300 is appropriate: big enough to discourage some limp-calls but not so large that I’m overcommitted if I face a 3-bet. If called I’ll use position to c-bet on dry, favorable flops and play cautiously on coordinated boards; if I face a large 3-bet (especially from deep-stacked Flash) I’ll re-evaluate and likely fold, whereas I may call a smaller 3-bet from Elon given his stack and my implied odds.

I am in the Small Blind with 9c 3s, which is a very weak hand. Minni raised from the Button to 300. Even though I have a large stack, calling out of position with such a weak hand against a raise is not a profitable strategy. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

Preflop in BB facing a button raise to 300 after SB fold. Minni's opening range from button is wide, likely 25-35% of hands including many broadways, suited connectors, Ax. QTo offsuit plays well against this range with good equity (~42% vs typical button open) and blockers to strong Q and T hands. Pot odds are favorable: 200 to call into 450 pot (pot becomes 650, needing ~23% equity, but real equity higher). OOP but deep enough stacks (46BB) to realize equity postflop. Folding too tight, 3-betting marginal without suit. Standard defend by calling to see flop.

Flop Ts Ks Kd gives me middle pair with Tc (pair of Tens) and Qs for minor backdoor spade draw/blocker. Board is extremely dry for Minni's wide button range: paired kings favor her Ax/Kx holdings heavily, but she also has overpairs QQ/JJ, flush draws (spades), straight draws (QJ), and bluffs. OOP first to act into 650 pot. Betting risks fold equity loss or getting raised; her range bets strong 70-80%+ here. Checking realizes equity vs her wide value/c-bet range (~45-55% equity) while controlling pot. Pot odds post-check bet favorable if small. Folding too tight preflop was marginal but now committed small; check to induce bluffs/value thin.

I raised preflop and am now heads-up on a dry but high board (Ts Ks Kd) with an underpair (44). As the preflop raiser I should c-bet to take the initiative and fold out ace-highs and other unpaired hands that missed — I have very little showdown value and only ~8.4% to hit a set by the river. A half-pot sizing gives good fold equity without overcommitting against Elon's 4,430 stack. If he folds I pick up the pot; if he calls I can control the pot on the turn and re-evaluate; if he raises or shoves I will give credit to a K/T or strong holding and fold. Bet half-pot to apply pressure and protect my equity.

Facing a 325 bet into 975 pot (~33% pot size) on Ts Ks Kd flop with Qs Tc (middle pair, Queen kicker, backdoor spade). Pot odds require ~25% equity to call profitably (325 into 1300). Minni's button range is wide (25-35%): AK, Kx, broadways, suited connectors, Ax. Paired Kings favor her overpairs/ Kx but she c-bets frequently with air, draws (spade flush draw possible), and thin value. My equity vs polarized c-bet range ~40-50% (beats bluffs, worse Kx like KJo, QJ; loses to top pair AK/KQ, sets). OOP but board extremely dry (few turns change dynamics badly; runner-runner straights unlikely). Calling realizes equity, keeps pot small vs thin value, induces bluffs on blank turns. Raising risks commitment with marginal strength, vulnerable to 4-bet shoves; folding too tight given odds and history of Minni's aggression (frequent button opens/c-bets, thin value). Standard profitable defend/call OOP.

On this turn 6c, a complete blank that doesn't complete any draws or improve many hands meaningfully, I hold middle pair (Tens) with Queen kicker and a useless Qs. The board Ts Ks Kd 6c remains paired kings, extremely dry, favoring Minni's button range which includes Kx (KQ, KJs, etc.), Ax, QQ/JJ overpairs, and bluffs/missed draws. Preflop defend was standard with QTo vs wide button open; flop check-call was correct OOP realizing equity vs her polarized c-bet range (~40-50% equity). Now OOP first to act into 1300 pot with 4105 stack (~3.2x pot). Betting risks folding out her worse hands (e.g., Ax, QQ, bluffs) while getting raised by better Kx/AK/sets; thin value bet gets called by worse but builds pot OOP awkwardly. Checking controls pot size, induces bluffs/value bets from thin Kx, and favorable pot odds to call continuation (~25-33% pot bets requiring ~20-25% equity, where we have ~45% vs her range). River will likely be playable with implied odds or check-down. Folding impossible. Raising absent. Check is GTO-optimal OOP defender spot to realize equity vs aggressive button.

Turn summary: I have pocket 4s on Ts Ks Kd 6c — an underpair with only 2 outs to hit a set on the river (2/46 ≈ 4.35%). Elon called my preflop raise and my flop c-bet, which weights his range toward Kx, Tx or other made hands that will happily call further. Betting here gains very little value, builds the pot for a hand with almost no equity, and risks inducing a shove from a shorter-stacked opponent that I should fold to. With the pot at 1,300 and my stack much deeper than Elon’s, I should control pot size and see the river for free. Plan: check to avoid bloating the pot; fold to any sizable bet on the turn; if checked through to the river I will only continue aggressively if I actually hit my set (or face a bet small enough to justify a bluff-call, which is unlikely).

River 7s completes the spade flush draw (Ts, Ks, 7s) and adds a low blank card, but the board remains paired kings (Ks Kd), making straights unlikely (no clear draws hit except flushes). I hold middle pair Tens (Tc) with Queen kicker (Qs spade blocker to some flushes like KQs/QJs), decent showdown value against Minni's wide button range post check-check turn: includes Kx (top pair like KQ/KJ/KT/AK but she may have slowed down), Ax, QQ/JJ overpairs, missed spade draws (now flushes, bad for us), and bluffs/A-high. Preflop defend/flop/turn calls were standard OOP realizing equity vs her aggression (~40-50% equity). Pot 1300, OOP first to act with 4105 stack (~3x pot). Betting thin value risks folding out worse (Ax, QQ, bluffs) while inviting raises from Kx/flushes/sets; pot odds unfavorable if raised. Checking maximizes EV: induces bluffs/value thin bets from Kx/Ax (call profitably OOP with ~25-33% equity needed vs pot bet, we have higher), controls pot, and showdowns vs air/overpairs we beat. River dynamics favor checkers; GTO-optimal OOP with marginal pair/blockers on flush-completing board.

Check. I have 4♣4♥ (pair of fours) on Ts Ks Kd 6c 7s and missed my set. Elon was the lone caller preflop and called my flop c-bet, which heavily weights his range toward Kx, Tx or other made hands that beat a lone 4. The river check to me is a free opportunity to control the pot — a bluff here has very limited fold equity against his calling line and could induce a shove from his short stack that I should fold to. Pot preservation and stack size matter more than trying to steal this spot, so I check and will fold to any meaningful bet; if checked through we go to showdown.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with King-Deuce suited. This is a standard opening hand from the button to put pressure on the blinds, especially as the chip leader. A 3x raise is consistent with my previous betting patterns and should either take down the blinds or allow me to play a pot in position.

Ts8s is a strong suited connector with good playability, straight and flush potential. Facing a button raise from Flash, who has been aggressive in prior hands (e.g., frequent raises from late position). Pot odds are favorable: calling 250 into 450 (pot will be ~700 post-call), offering about 2:1 direct odds plus strong implied odds due to hand's potential. SB defense range includes suited connectors vs button opens. OOP post-flop but can check-fold weak boards or play aggressively on favorable ones. Minni in BB may isolate or fold, but multiway potential is okay with this hand. Folding is too tight; 3-betting speculative and commits awkwardly OOP. Prefer calling to realize equity.

I’ll call (put a total of 300 in). Folding A♣3♥ (actually A♥3♣) to a standard 3x button open in a 3-handed game is too tight and Flash has been opening wide; Elon has already defended, so this will likely be a multiway pot where my Ace-high has reasonable equity. A cold 3-bet is unnecessary and risky: A3o has a weak kicker and is often crushed by other Aces or gets into a bloated pot out of position. Calling costs 200 more for ~22% break-even odds (pot 700 -> 900 if I call), lets me see a flop cheaply, and preserve stack depth to play postflop if I hit an Ace or a favorable texture. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet preflop, on the flop proceed cautiously — value bet/top pair thinly but avoid committing with only Ace-kicker vs heavy action.

Flop of 6d-5c-Js hits me with a gutshot straight draw to the 7 (for 5-6-7-8-Ts), backdoor spade flush draw, and a Ten as a live overcard. No pair or strong draw. Pot is 900 multiway (me SB OOP first to act, aggressive Flash on button who raised pre, Minni in BB). Betting thin risks raising from better hands or getting called OOP with marginal equity. Checking controls the pot, induces bets from Flash's wide button range (which includes many floats/bluffs), allows realization of implied odds if I hit, and I can fold to aggression if needed while preserving stack (5105). Folding isn't an option post-call; raising impossible without bet. Standard check to see action.

Check. I have A♥3♣ — just Ace-high on a J♠-6♦-5♣ rainbow board with no real draw (only one club). This is a multiway spot vs a wide button opener and a defending SB, so betting for fold equity is thin and I’m usually getting called by better Aces or pairs. Pot is 900 and my stack is deep enough to wait for value; I’ll let opponents show aggression and only call small continuation bets to see a turn. Plan: check-call small bets (~<1/3 pot) but fold to sizable pressure unless I improve to top pair or a clear two-card draw; if the street checks through I’ll show down my Ace-high.

I started the action pre-flop and both blinds called. The flop of 6d-5c-Js is relatively dry but has some low connectivity. Both opponents checked to me. While I only have King-high and a backdoor flush draw, I have the positional advantage and the betting initiative. A continuation bet of 450 (half-pot) is appropriate here to represent a Jack or a high pocket pair and to force out hands that missed the flop, such as lower connectors or un-paired overcards.

Facing a 450 bet into 1350 pot on 6d5cJs flop with Ts8s (gutshot straight draw to 7, backdoor spade flush draw, live Ten overcard). Pot odds are about 3:1 (needing ~23% equity), but multiway OOP after checking, equity realization is poor. Flash's button range is wide (suited connectors, broadways, Ax suited), and this bet continuation range includes many Jx, sets, two-pairs, combo draws (e.g., 74s, 74o), and floats. My hand blocks few strong hands but gets dominated often by better draws/straights (e.g., 47, 74, T7). Calling commits ~9% of stack OOP with marginal equity (~20-25% vs polarized range) and low implied odds due to reverse implied if hit (villain likely stronger). Minni checked behind pre-flop and flop, weak range, but multiway worsens my position. Raising has poor fold equity against aggressive Flash (25k stack). Discipline calls for folding speculative draw missing flop OOP; preserve stack for better spots. Consistent with prior check to induce and control pot.

Board J♠-6♦-5♣, I hold A♥3♣. Flash opened and c-bet 450 into a 900 pot (50% pot). Calling 450 to win a pot of 1800 requires ~25% equity. My hand has almost no clean draws — only backdoor club possibilities and a few marginal pair-outs (3s/Aces) that are often behind continuing ranges. Flash has been opening wide but on this flop after two checks a large c-bet still represents a lot of value (Jx, overpairs, pair+draws) and I’m out of position, so I’ll face difficult turns if I call. I planned to check-call very small c-bets (<~1/3 pot) but fold to sizable pressure; this is that sizable bet. Fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

Preflop on the button with Q6o after SB folds. Dead money in the pot (150 total, including SB's 50). Excellent position heads-up against BB. Q6o plays well postflop in position due to queen high and decent playability. High fold equity on a raise as BB defends wide but not always. Raising steals the pot often or builds it when ahead. Size to 3x BB (300 total) for standard button vs blind sizing. Avoids limping which is weak OOP potential.

Elon opened to 300 (pot 450); I have 8c6s in the small blind and face 250 to call. This is a weak, unsuited, gap hand that does poorly multi‑way and will be out of position postflop (I act before both opponents). Calling 250 into 450 is expensive for a hand that is often dominated and gives poor realization of equity, and a min‑raise to 500 would be a thin, speculative play that can be re‑raised or isolate me OOP. Preserve my stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Nine of spades, a strong suited connector in three-handed play. Elon, the button, has raised to 300. Minni in the Small Blind has folded. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, giving me very favorable pot odds of 3.25 to 1. Given the strength of my hand and the position, calling is the most standard and effective play to see a flop and utilize the playability of my suited connectors.

I have an open-ended straight draw (King or Eight) on a paired board of Queen-Ten-Queen. While the paired board increases the risk of an opponent holding a Queen for trips or better, my hand has significant drawing potential. In this three-handed game, checking to the pre-flop aggressor (Elon) is the standard play to see how he reacts to this Broadway-heavy board, which likely connects with his button opening range.

The flop Qh Ts Qd is a dream board for my Qs6h, giving me top set (trip Queens). Paired board reduces villain's straight draw possibilities but opens flush draws or weaker pairs. Flash checked after calling preflop raise, representing a wide range including Ax suited, broadway, pocket pairs, suited connectors. In position with a monster, betting for value is mandatory to build the pot and charge draws/pairs. Sizing to ~2/3 pot (450) balances value extraction with protection against sets or two-pair combos, while keeping SPR reasonable postflop. Avoid checking to induce bluffs or get check-raised off equity.

I have an open-ended straight draw (8s and Kings) on a paired board of Qh-Ts-Qd. Elon's bet of 450 into a 700 pot gives me roughly 2.5 to 1 odds. With 8 outs to a straight and some backdoor flush potential with my spades, calling is the most mathematically sound decision here. Raising is too risky given the board is paired, as Elon could easily have a Queen or a better hand that won't fold, while folding is too passive given the strength of my draw in a three-handed game.

On the turn, the 5 of clubs is a total blank. I still hold an open-ended straight draw (needs a King or an Eight) on a paired board. Since I am out of position and Elon was the pre-flop and flop aggressor, checking is the most prudent play. This allows me to see if Elon checks behind, giving me a free card to hit my straight, or to evaluate the size of his bet if he continues. Leading here would likely only be called by better hands (Queens, Tens, or high pairs) and wouldn't accomplish much as a bluff given the board texture.

Top set of Queens remains an absolute monster on this Qh Ts Qd 5c board. The turn 5c is a relatively blank card that doesn't complete many strong draws (no flush, straight draws like KJ or 98 were already live but not improved significantly). Flash has checked twice—once on the flop and now on turn—after defending wide preflop from the BB and calling my flop c-bet. His range includes weaker Qx (AJ-QJ suited/off), Tx (offsuit suited), pocket pairs below Q, straight draws (e.g., J9, 89), and floats. He could have two-pair (QT, Q5 unlikely) or even a set (TT, but less likely). No reason to check behind with the nuts; continue value betting to extract from his wide calling range. Betting also protects against any villainous bluffs or improves. Pot is 1550; sizing to ~3/4 pot (1100) builds the pot towards my effective stack of 4355 while keeping SPR low (~2:1 post-bet) and allowing a river shove if called. Avoids overbetting into potential traps while maximizing value from worse hands.

On the turn, the board is Queen-Ten-Queen-Five. I have an open-ended straight draw (King or Eight). Elon, who has been the aggressor throughout the hand, has bet 1100 into a pot of 1550. This represents roughly 70% of the pot. To call, I need 1100 to win a total potential pot of 3750, which requires approximately 29.3% equity. My 8 outs to a straight give me roughly 17.4% equity. While implied odds exist since Elon has 3255 chips remaining, the paired board (Queens) significantly devalues my draw. If Elon has a Queen, he has trips, and if the board pairs further or if he holds Q-T, Q-5, or pocket Tens, my straight could be drawing dead or second-best. Given the high cost of the call relative to the probability of hitting a clean out on a dangerous board, folding is the most disciplined and stack-preserving move.

Button with Tc8d in a 3-handed game is a playable opener — I want initiative and to capitalize on position. Standard open size here is ~3x the BB; raising to 300 (total) applies pressure to the big blind and the deep-stacked small blind, gives fold equity, and keeps pot control with 84bbs behind. Plan: if I get a single caller play postflop aggressively on dry, favorable boards (continuation-bet), be cautious on coordinated/ace-high flops, and fold to a sizable 3-bet or shove (T8o has poor equity vs a polarized 3-bet). If called and action goes multiway, slow down and look for favorable turn cards before committing more chips.

I am in the Small Blind holding Jack of Diamonds and Ten of Spades, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. Calling is a viable option to see a flop with a hand that has good playability and straight potential, especially since I'm getting decent pot odds (250 to call into a pot of 450). Raising is also an option, but given the stack sizes and position, calling allows me to keep the pot manageable while playing a hand that flops well.

Preflop in the big blind with 8s5d, a speculative offsuit hand with poor connectivity and no high card value. Facing a button raise from Minni (who raises frequently) and a small blind call from Flash, the hand has negligible equity against their combined ranges. Postflop playability is awful multiway, and while pot odds are ~3:1 for a 200 call into 700, the EV is negative long-term due to dominated equity and difficult spots ahead. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser on a board that is very coordinated and favors their range. The flop is monotone (three clubs), and I hold no clubs and only a weak gutshot straight draw (needing a King). I have no showdown value and very little equity to continue if faced with a bet. Checking is the standard play here to see how the opponent reacts.

Flop: Ac 8c Qc; I have Tc 8d — middle pair (pair of eights) with one club in hand (4 clubs known total), so I have a single-card flush draw (9 outs, ~35% to make by the river) but I do NOT have a made club flush. This is a highly coordinated, dangerous flop: three clubs plus two overcards (A and Q) that hit a large portion of Flash’s calling range. Flash has shown a tendency to call postflop a lot, and his range from the small blind will include many two-club combos, Ax/Qx hands and broadway holdings that dominate or severely outdraw my hand.

Given position and pot control, I prefer to check. A c-bet here will frequently be called by hands that already crush me (flushes, A/Qx) or by sticky draws, losing value and bloating the pot out of position. By checking I keep the pot manageable, gather information, and can fold to heavy action vs likely made hands. Plan: if Flash checks back I take a free turn card and can barrel or bet appropriately on safe turns; if he bets small (roughly <=1/2 pot ≈ 350) I will call with my pair + draw to realize equity; if he fires a large bet or pot-sized bet I will assume a made flush/strong Ax/Qx and fold. If I improve to two pair or make the flush on the turn, I will proceed for value/raise.

On this monotone board (Ac 8c Qc), I have very little equity with Jd Ts and no club in hand. Minni checked back the flop, which could indicate a range of hands from weak pairs to trapped flushes, but more likely signifies a hand with some showdown value that doesn't want to bet into a potentially made flush. The 9h turn doesn't improve my hand. Checking again is the best play; I am not in a good position to represent a hand, and I'd rather take a free card to try and hit a King for a straight or simply fold if faced with a significant bet.

I check. I hold Tc8d on Ac 8c Qc 9h — a single pair (eights) with a one-card club draw. On the turn I have 9 club outs to hit a flush on the river (9/46 ≈ 19.6%), plus 3 remaining tens and 2 remaining eights (14 unique outs total, ~30% to improve to two‑pair/trips/flush). Pot is only 700 and my stack is deep (8120). Flash checked flop and checked the turn, showing range weakness or a desire to control the pot; he has shown a tendency to check-call small/medium bets in prior hands. Betting the turn here would bloat the pot with a marginal made hand and will frequently get called by better hands (A/Qx) or by sticky draws, while folding out some worse hands I actually want to keep (I want hands that pay me off if I hit the river). Checking preserves pot control, keeps my equity alive to the river, and lets me extract more value or avoid big losses depending on the river card. Plan: take the free river card — if a club hits I will size for value; if the river misses and Flash bets small I'll call to realize equity, but I will respect large-sized bets/raises that polarize to made flushes or strong Ax/Qx.

The river pairs the Nine, but the board remains dominated by the three clubs from the flop. I have Jack-high with no clubs. Minni has checked back twice, suggesting she likely has a marginal showdown hand like a weak Ace, a Queen, or a small pocket pair that was playing cautiously against a potential flush. Since I have no showdown value, I could consider a bluff, but the pot is small (700) and Minni's line often results in a 'calling-station' mode on the river if I bet small. Checking is the most prudent play to see if she checks back or to fold if she bets, as I cannot win the hand unless she is holding total air like 76s.

I check. I hold T♣8♦ for a single pair of eights (board A♣ 8♣ Q♣ 9♥ 9♠). This river is a very wet/coordinated board (three clubs, A/Q/paired 9s) that contains many hands which beat me (A‑x, Q‑x, club flushes, pocket 9s) and a fair number of hands that will not fold to a river bet. Flash checked flop/turn/river and has shown a tendency to check‑call small/medium bets — a thin bet here is likely to get called by better hands or fold out only the hands I beat, so it has low positive EV. Pot is small (700) and my stack is deep (8120), so pot control and preserving chips for higher EV spots is important. Checking and taking the showdown is the least risk and highest EV line here.

In this 3-handed scenario, I am on the button with Qd 4c. While being on the button usually warrants a wide opening range, Queen-Four offsuit is near the bottom of that range and is generally considered too weak to open effectively, especially with relatively short-stacked opponents who may look to shove or defend aggressively. Maintaining my chip lead is a priority, and there's no reason to enter a pot with such a marginal hand.

Preflop in the small blind with Tc8s offsuit against the big blind after button folds. Tc8s is a weak offsuit gapped hand with poor playability out of position. While pot odds to complete the blind are favorable (calling 50 into 150), the hand lacks strong equity, blockers, or suitedness to justify playing OOP against Minni's likely wide defending range. Folding avoids difficult postflop spots and preserves stack for better opportunities. Raising is too loose with this holding given stacks and history of aggressive play.

3-handed on the button first to act pre-flop with A9 suited, a strong premium hand with good playability, position, and equity against calling ranges. Pot is 150 (blinds), to call 100. Standard open-raise to 3x the BB (300 total) to steal blinds or build pot OOP with initiative and position advantage. Opponents have shown aggression historically but this spot is too good to limp or fold. My stack of 5755 allows comfortable raise sizing without overcommitting.

I’m in the small blind with 8♣5♣ facing a 300 button open. With a deep stack (~81 BB) this is a cheap price to see a flop and 8♣5♣ has playability (suited, potential straight/flush equity) against a wide button opening range. I considered a 3-bet, but this hand is a pure bluff preflop (no relevant blockers), Flash behind is very deep and can 3/4-bet or call light, and I’ll be out of position postflop — so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Folding is too tight given the small additional cost (250 chips). Plan: call to realize equity; on the flop play cautiously (fold to heavy aggression on dry boards, continue/c-bet or semi-bluff when I have clubs or a good draw, extract value when I pair/complete strong draws).

I am in the Big Blind with Ace of Diamonds and Six of Diamonds. Elon raised to 300 from the Button, and Minni called from the Small Blind. The price to call is only 200 more into a pot of 700, giving me excellent pot odds of 4.5:1. Suited Aces play well in multi-way pots, and since I have a massive chip lead, I can afford to see a flop and look to outplay my opponents post-flop or hit a strong draw. While a squeeze is an option, calling is a lower-variance play that realizes the equity of this hand efficiently.

Check. I have 8♣5♣ on 4♥ A♠ 6♥ — no pair, only a 4‑out gutshot to a 7 and a backdoor club flush (needs two cards). This is a multiway pot (button opened, BB called) and I’m out of position, so I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal hand.
