I called preflop for cheap equity/playability; on this dry, Ace‑high flop pot control is correct. Plan: check and fold to significant aggression, but consider calling very small bets (≈≤1/3 pot ≈300) since the implied odds and two-card equity can justify it. If the turn gives me a 7, a pair, or a club draw I’ll become more proactive.

I have flopped top two pair (Aces and Sixes) on a board that contains a flush draw (two hearts) and some straight possibilities. While checking to the pre-flop aggressor (Elon) is a standard play, in a three-way pot, I want to ensure I start building the pot immediately and charge any draws (like heart draws or gutshots). Since Minni has already checked, a bet of 500 (just over half-pot) targets value from any Ace-x hands in their ranges and protects my hand against potential turn cards that could complete draws or complicate the board.

Flop board 4h As 6h gives me top pair aces with Ah pairing the As, plus the nut flush draw holding four hearts (Ah 9h 6h 4h). This is an extremely strong hand with massive equity against Flash's likely bluff/draw/catch range after his half-pot bet from the BB following my preflop button raise. Minni checked UTG, likely weak. Pot odds to call are great (1400 into 500), but raising extracts value from Flash's worse draws, pairs, and floats while protecting against straight draws (53) or runner-runner threats. Position allows control. Flash's history shows aggression (e.g., Hand 2 massive preflop, frequent bets), but his huge stack (25k) means he can continue lighter; however, my hand crushes his range. Raising to 1500 (3x his bet) sizes well into 1400 pot, ~1/3 pot raise overall, balances value and semi-bluff protection. Stack depth (5455) supports without overcommitting. Folding or calling too passive with this monster.

I called preflop with 8♣5♣ to realize equity. The flop A♠‑4♥‑6♥ is very dry for my hand (only a 7 gives me a straight; backdoor club flush needs two cards). Flash leads with 500 and Elon raises to 1500. The pot is 2900 and it costs 1500 to call, so I’d need roughly 1500/(2900+1500) ≈ 34% equity to break even. My realistic equity here is well under that versus Elon’s raising range (and even worse multiway vs both opponents): I only have 4 clean outs to a 7 to make the straight and a few weak pair-outs that will often still lose to Ax or sets. I’m out of position to two aggressive players and there’s a real chance of facing more action. Given the price, board texture, ranges and stack dynamics, folding preserves chips for better spots — I fold.

I have flopped top two pair (Aces and Sixes) on a relatively wet board with a flush draw present. After leading into the pot, Elon has raised, which typically signifies a strong range including hands like Ace-King, Ace-Queen, or possibly a set (44 or 66). Given that Elon has a relatively short stack (3,955 remaining) and the pot is already substantial, the SPR (Stack-to-Pot Ratio) is very low. By raising now, I commit Elon to the pot with his top-pair hands or strong draws while I am likely ahead, and I protect my hand against potential heart turn cards that could either complete a draw or kill the action. I will raise to 5,455, which covers Elon's remaining chips and puts him all-in.

Facing an all-in 4-bet shove from Flash after my flop raise with top pair top kicker (Ah pairing As) plus the nut flush draw on a coordinated 4h-As-6h board heavy with draws. My hand crushes Flash's likely polarized range: value like sets (44/66), two-pair (A6/A4/64), or bluffs/missed draws (Kh flush draw, gutshots, air). Flash's BB defending range vs button raise is wide, and his aggression history (Hand 2: massive preflop 4-bet shove; Hands 15/27/35: frequent postflop bets/raises light; often checks/folds to pressure) supports bluffs here after checking initially then donk-betting and jamming over raise. Pot odds: calling 3955 into 7855 (~2:1, need 33% equity). Equity analysis: vs nuts/set ~25-35% (9 flush outs + 3-6 pair/set outs); vs worse pairs/draws >50%; overall >40% vs polarized range. Monster with huge implied/playability, deep opponent stack post-call irrelevant as all-in. No reason to fold premium hand OOP with direct pot odds met and history favoring call.

4♠9♥ is a very weak, unsuited, poorly-connected holding. The pot is 150 and it costs 100 to see a flop, so I need ~28.6% equity to make a call profitable. Multiway (vs SB and BB) my raw equity with 4-9o will be well under that and I’ll be dominated a lot. I could open as a steal, but both opponents have deep stacks and have shown willingness to defend and 3‑bet — exposing me to a difficult decision with a junk hand. Avoiding marginal, multiway postflop spots and preserving my ~78bb stack is the best play here.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Four offsuit. The Button (Minni) has folded, leaving just me and the Big Blind (Elon). K4o is a marginal hand, but in a blind-versus-blind situation, it is strong enough to put pressure on the opponent. I have a significant chip lead (20,270) and Elon has 11,710. A standard raise to 3x the big blind often takes down the pot immediately or allows me to take control of the hand. I will raise to 300.

Td5h is a trash hand: offsuit, poorly connected, ten-high with weak kicker. From BB facing SB raise to 300 after BTN fold, this is a classic steal spot, but T5o has terrible post-flop playability, low equity, and no fold equity if shoving or raising. Standard play is to fold weak hands like this to preserve stack for better spots. Opponents are aggressive but no reason to defend here. Pot odds ~2:1 but hand too weak.

I am on the button with 8c 5d. This is a very weak offsuit hand. While being on the button provides a positional advantage, this hand does not have enough equity or playability to justify a raise or a call against the blinds, especially early in a tournament where I want to preserve my stack for higher-equity situations. I will fold and wait for a better hand.

Preflop in SB vs BB heads-up after Button fold. A3o is a strong stealing hand from SB due to Ace blocker reducing opponent's premium Ax hands, fold equity against BB's wide folding range, and decent postflop playability with overcard and potential to hit top pair. Standard play is to raise wide; limping is suboptimal as it invites raises and plays out of position weakly. Raising 3x BB to 300 applies pressure, risks only ~2.6% of stack, and builds pot when ahead. Minni's history shows frequent raising but variable defense; high chance of fold here. Folding wastes SB; this maximizes EV.

I’m in the big blind with K♣T♣ facing a 3x small‑blind open to 300. Heads‑up KTs is a strong, well‑connected suited Broadway that does well both for value and postflop play. Calling surrenders initiative and gives Elon position on every postflop street; folding is too tight. I’ll 3‑bet to take the initiative and get fold equity against his wide SB open. I choose a sizing of 1,200 total — big enough to apply pressure and get value from worse broadways/Ax while leaving healthy stacks (~77BB effective) to play postflop. If he 4‑bets I’ll re‑evaluate; if he calls I’ll play the flop for position and equity.

Facing a 3-bet from Minni in the BB after my standard SB steal raise to 300 with A3o. A3o has Ace-blocker value reducing her AA/AK likelihood, but poor postflop playability OOP with weak kicker and offsuit. Pot 1500, to-call 900 (pot odds ~37.5% equity needed). Vs credible BB 3-bet range (QQ+, AKo/s, AQo/s, JJ+), equity ~25-35% max, often dominated. Minni aggressive historically (frequent raises/3-bets), unlikely bluff here vs steal. Calling commits ~8% stack into low SPR pot (~4:1 post-call), vulnerable to further aggression. 4-betting risks 25%+ stack with marginal hand. Fold preserves stack for superior positions/150-hand tournament.

3-handed on the button with Qd5s offsuit. Q5o is a playable hand for a raise from the button in position, especially against potentially weak blind ranges. Pot is 150 with 100 to call (likely SB limped or posted blinds with no further action), offering good implied odds but raising builds the pot in position and has fold equity. Opponents have shown willingness to fold preflop in history (many folds shown). Standard button raise sizing is 3x the BB to 300. Deep stacks allow for postflop playability with position. Folding too tight; calling multiway weak. Raise to isolate and steal.

I’m in the small blind with J♣8♥ facing a button open to 300. I’ve already posted 50, so calling requires 250 more (300 total) into a 450 pot — good price to see a flop with a marginal but playable hand. J8o can make top pair, second pair, and backdoor draws; 3-betting as a bluff here is riskier because the big blind (Flash) is deep and likely to defend, and I don’t want to take a dominated spot out of position. Folding is too tight given the pot odds. Plan: call, see the flop, continue with pairs or strong draws, and fold to significant aggression without improvement.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Trey offsuit. Elon raised to 300 from the Button and Minni called from the Small Blind. Although I am getting good pot odds to call (200 chips to win a 700 chip pot), K3o is a very weak hand to play multi-way and out of position. The likelihood of being dominated or unable to realize my equity on the flop is high. Given my current stack lead, I prefer to fold and wait for better opportunities rather than risking chips in a marginal spot.

I called preflop with J♣8♥ to see a flop — the As 2d 9c completely misses my hand: no pair, no realistic flush (only two clubs possible by river) and only remote runner-runner straight backdoors. I’m out of position to the original raiser (Elon) whose range contains many Aces and broadways and who will c-bet frequently. Pot control is important here: I’ll check to avoid bloating the pot with air and to see if he checks back. Plan: fold to a standard continuation bet (or any sizable aggression) and only continue if he checks or I improve on the turn (pair of J/8 or a meaningful draw).

Heads-up on the flop after my button raise and Minni's call from SB. Board is As2d9c, rainbow, ace-high. My Qd5s is queen-high with no pair or strong draw, but as the preflop raiser in position, this is a standard spot for a continuation bet. Minni checked, showing weakness, and her range includes many hands that miss this board (e.g., low pairs, suited connectors below 9). History shows Minni often calls pre but folds postflop to aggression. C-betting takes advantage of fold equity and represents an ace or strong hand. Pot 700, sizing to ~50-60% (400) denies equity to draws and builds pot if called. If she folds, easy pot; if calls, reassess turn with position and deep stacks. Checking risks giving free cards to her draws or letting her realize equity.

I checked the flop as planned and missed completely on A♠‑2♦‑9♣ with J♣8♥ — no pair and no meaningful draws. Elon’s 400 into a 1,100 pot is a standard continuation bet. Calling 400 gives roughly 21% pot odds and my chance to make at least a pair by the river (~24%) is borderline, but versus Elon’s c‑bet range (lots of A‑x and warranted continuation hands) my real equity and post‑flop playability OOP are poor. Raising as a bluff is thin — I have weak blockers and would be playing out of position against deeper stacks. I’ll preserve my ~78bb stack and fold to this c‑bet, waiting for a better spot to invest chips.

On the button in a 3-handed pot I should open a fairly wide range to take the initiative and steal the blinds. Q♠4♠ (Q4o) is marginal but acceptable for a button open — I want fold equity and to avoid letting the blinds see a cheap flop. I will raise to 300 (≈3x BB), which is a standard, practical sizing here and consistent with my previous opens. Post-flop plan: if everyone folds I pick up the blinds; if I'm called I'll proceed cautiously — c-bet when I hit or when the flop texture favors a continuation, check and control the pot when I miss. If either blind 3-bets large, I will fold Q4o (only consider calling a very small 3-bet versus a specific opponent read).

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Eight offsuit. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. While I have a significant chip lead, J8o is a weak hand to play out of position against a button raise. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop, and 3-betting is unnecessary with such a marginal hand. The most disciplined play to maintain my stack advantage is to fold.

Ts3c is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability postflop, low equity against Minni's button opening range which is wide but still includes many better hands that dominate mine. Facing a standard 3x raise, the pot odds to call 200 into 450 require about 30% equity, but T3o realizes far less against typical button ranges due to being offsuit, disconnected, and dominated often. No strong blockers or speculative value. Folding from BB is standard to preserve stack for premium spots in a tournament setting with 3 players left and deep stacks relative to blinds. Minni's history shows frequent aggression, but this isn't a profitable defend.

I have been dealt pocket Kings (Kh Kc) on the button. This is the second-best starting hand in Texas Hold'em and a premium situation. My goal is to build a large pot and get value from weaker hands. Standard opening size in this game has been 300, and given the stack sizes and the strength of my hand, I will raise to 300 to induce action while maintaining a consistent betting pattern.

Q4o is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, no suited potential, straight draws, or pair value. Facing a button raise from Flash, who has been aggressive in prior hands, the correct play is to fold preflop from the small blind. Pot odds are not sufficient (calling 250 into 450 gives ~1.8:1), and my equity against a typical raising range is too low. Preserving stack for better spots in a tournament setting.

Call 300 total (put 200 more in). Qh2h is marginal but suited and has some playability from the big blind vs a button open. The pot is 450 and the call is cheap (200 to call), so pot odds justify defending. Flash has been opening frequently to 300, so his range is relatively wide — calling lets me realize equity with backdoor hearts and occasional top-pair Q without committing out of position. A min-raise to 500 would be overly ambitious with this hand and would leave me OOP; folding would be too tight given the price. I'll call and evaluate postflop.

Check. I defended preflop with Q♥2♥ vs a wide button open, but the flop A♥ T♦ 8♣ heavily favors the raiser’s range (lots of A-x, T-x, 8-x and broadway hands). My holding has almost no showdown value and only backdoor heart/straight potential, so betting as a bluff OOP would have poor fold equity and risk bloating the pot with a marginal hand. With ~77bb effective I prefer pot control: check and see how Flash responds. Plan: call small continuation bets (cheap to realize backdoor equity), fold to large C-bets or raises, and take a free card if he checks back.

I have pocket Kings (Kh Kc), which is a very strong hand. However, the flop (Ah Td 8c) contains an Ace, which is the most dangerous card for my range. Since I raised pre-flop, Minni's check is expected. I need to balance between betting for protection and checking for control. If I bet and get raised, I am in a difficult spot. If I check, I can keep the pot small and potentially catch bluffs or extract thin value later. However, given my position and the need to protect against straight draws (like QJ or 97), a small continuation bet can help define her hand and take down the pot if she doesn't have an Ace. Since the board is somewhat coordinated, I will bet small (approx 40% pot) to see where I stand.

Flash (button) has been opening very wide and the flop bet is small (250 into a 900 pot). After his bet the pot will be 1,150, so calling 250 gives ~21.7% pot odds. Q♥2♥ has almost no showdown value on A♥ T♦ 8♣ — only runner-runner heart possibilities and occasional showdown vs a wide bluffing segment — so raising OOP would be too ambitious and folding is too tight given the price. I’ll take the cheap call to realize equity and see a turn card. Plan: fold to a sizeable raise, call small turn bets to continue when I pick up a heart or a queen, and otherwise keep the pot controlled.

Turn (5♣) kills my backdoor heart plan — I no longer have a realistic flush draw and Q♥2♥ is now almost never good versus a preflop raising/button c‑bet range that contains many A‑x, T‑x and broadways. Pot is 1,150 and there is nothing to call right now. Betting OOP with queen‑high would bloat the pot with very little fold equity and leave me in a tough spot if raised. I’ll keep pot control and check to see the river for a cheap shot at a pair (3 queens + 3 deuces outs) or to fold to pressure. If the button checks back on the river I can consider a small blocker bet, but I will fold to any meaningful river aggression without improvement.

I have pocket Kings, but the board features an Ace which was present since the flop. I bet the flop to see where I stood and Minni called, suggesting she has at least a piece of the board or a draw. The turn 5c is relatively blank, although it brings a back-door flush draw. By checking behind here, I am exercising pot control. If Minni has an Ace, I don't want to build a bigger pot. If she is on a draw or has a weaker pair (like a Ten or an Eight), checking allows me to potentially see a cheap showdown or induce a bluff on the river. Betting again risks a check-raise that would force me to fold a hand with decent showdown value.

Pot is 1,150 and I hold Q♥2♥ — queen-high only with no meaningful showdown value. Flash opened wide and c‑bet small on the flop, then both of us checked the turn; that line contains a lot of A‑x, T‑x, missed broadways and occasional bluffs. The river paired the Ten, which improves many hands in his range (and makes bluffs less attractive). Betting as a bluff OOP here has very poor fold equity and would bloat a pot I’m unlikely to win. I’ll take the safe line: check and concede the pot to any meaningful river aggression. If he checks back we show down, otherwise fold to a river bet.

I have pocket Kings, which is a very strong hand, but the board contains an Ace and a pair of Tens. Minni called my pre-flop raise and my flop continuation bet, then checked the turn and the river. While it's possible she is playing an Ace or a Ten passively, her repeated checking suggests she might have a weaker hand like an Eight or a missed draw. However, since an Ace or a Ten beats me, and it is unlikely she will call a bet with a hand worse than Kings, checking back is the most prudent play to see the showdown and preserve my stack.

3-handed on the button, first to act preflop with KJo offsuit. This is a strong opening hand in this format: king-high, broadway potential, blocks strong kings. Players have been raising to 300 consistently from early positions, so standard 3x open to 300 builds the pot, takes initiative, and leverages position. Minni (SB) and Flash (BB) defend wide 3-handed, but fold equity is high. Limp or fold too passive; raising maximizes EV.

Facing a button open to 300 from the SB with Th–7d (offsuit). Pot is 450 and I must call 250 more; my stack is ~74 BB. T7o is a weak, often-dominated hand and plays poorly out of position against both the opener and the big blind. 3-betting as a bluff here is unattractive (no suit/blockers and BB can over-call), and calling commits chips to a marginal postflop spot. Preserve stack and wait for a better, positionally favorable spot—fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 4h 5c. Elon has raised to 300 from the Button. While I have decent pot odds to call (200 chips to call into a 450 pot), 4-5 offsuit is a very weak hand that realizes equity poorly out of position. Given Elon's active play, I expect to be put in difficult spots post-flop with a range disadvantage. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Button vs SB/BB, folded to me with T♠2♥. This is a weak hand, but in 3-handed play the button's positional advantage justifies an open raise to steal the blinds and take the initiative. I’ll raise to 300 (3BB) — big enough to have fold equity and discourage limp-calls, small enough to preserve stack depth for postflop play. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold this hand; if called I’ll c-bet favorable textures (T-pairs, low paired or drawy boards) and check/control on dry boards. Calling 100 is too passive and a min-raise to 200 is too small and invites aggression, so 300 is the right size here.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s 5s. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. While 9s 5s is suited, it is quite weak and playing it out of position against both the button and the big blind is not ideal. Since I am the chip leader, there is no need to take marginal risks with speculative hands that perform poorly when called or reraised. Folding is the safest and most disciplined play here.

K4o is a weak offsuit hand with poor post-flop playability, especially out of position in the BB facing a button raise. Minni's button raise is likely wide, but calling 200 into 450 offers insufficient odds for this marginal holding that struggles against better kings or pairs. Folding preserves stack for better spots early in the tournament.

I am on the button with a strong suited hand (Qs 9s) in a three-handed game. My stack is the largest, and both opponents have relatively shorter stacks, especially Minni. A standard opening raise of 3x the big blind (300) is appropriate here to assert control, potentially take down the blinds immediately, or see a flop with initiative.

Preflop in small blind facing a button raise to 300 (pot 450, to call 250). Hand is Kc2s, offsuit, poor connectivity, dominated by any king in villain's range, and weak overall. Button raiser (Flash) likely wide but still profitable only with better holdings. Positionally disadvantaged OOP postflop. Pot odds ~1.8:1 require too much equity (~36%), but K2o has poor equity vs typical button open range (~25-30% at best, often less with reverse implied odds). Clear fold to preserve stack for better spots. No history this hand justifies calling.

I’m in the big blind (BB) with K♦8♥ facing a button open to 300. I only need to call 200 more (total commit 300) into a 450 pot. Calling 200 into a 450 pot gives me ~30.8% pot odds, and vs a typical BTN opening range K8o has enough equity heads-up to defend. Stacks are deep enough (7,470) that the price is small and I can fold postflop if I miss. I don’t want to 3-bet OOP with a marginal offsuit K — it’s a thin spot that creates tougher decisions and risks unnecessary escalation. Plan: call to see a flop; proceed aggressively if I hit (top pair, strong draw), play cautiously and fold to heavy continuation betting on dry boards.

Flop is dry and low (2d 7s 5c). I only have Kd8h — just an overcard with no flush draw and only remote backdoor straight chances. I called preflop planning to fold to heavy continuation betting on a dry board, so I want to keep the pot small and realize equity. Checking controls the pot, avoids tough OOP decisions, and lets me fold to a large c-bet while calling small c-bets to see a turn (call small bets up to ~1/3 pot ≈ 200–250; fold to ~1/2+ pot sizing or raises). If Flash checks back I get a free turn to pick up equity or barrel when I improve.

I opened the button with Qs 9s and was called by Minni in the big blind. The flop comes 2d 7s 5c, which is quite dry. Minni checks to me. While I haven't hit this board directly, I have two overcards and a backdoor spade draw. In a 3-handed game, this board texture favors the pre-flop aggressor as it's unlikely the big blind hit it hard. A standard continuation bet of about half the pot should be enough to take it down or define where I stand. I will bet 300.

Call 300. I’m OOP in the BB with K♦8♥ on a very dry 2♦-7♠-5♣ flop. Flash’s 300 into the 650 preflop pot (950 now) is a small c-bet (~32% of the pot). Calling 300 to see a turn requires only ~24% equity (300/(950+300)), and K8o has reasonable equity vs a BTN open/continuation range plus backdoor outs. I don’t want to 3‑bet OOP with a marginal offsuit K — that bloats the pot and creates difficult spots. With deep stacks I can see a turn cheaply and fold to significant aggression if I miss. Plan: call to keep the pot under control; fold to strong turn/river pressure unless I improve (pair a K or 8 or pick up a meaningful draw), and consider betting/raising only if I hit or get a good read.

I called the small flop c-bet to keep the pot controllable and planned to fold the turn unless I improved. The turn J hearts does not help K♦8♥ — no pair, no meaningful draw — and actually helps the opener's range (Jx, two-pair/sets are possible, plus many overcards and continue-candidates). Pot is 1,250 and I am OOP with deep stacks; leading out as a bluff here has limited fold equity against a wide but still-calling BTN range and creates tough spot later. Checking preserves stack, realizes my (very small) showdown equity, and keeps the pot small so I can fold to substantial turn/river aggression. Plan: check and fold to significant bet/raise on the turn; if Flash checks back I can evaluate the river with the option to bluff or check-call depending on the card and his action.

The turn Jack of Hearts is an overcard to the flop but doesn't particularly improve my hand. Minni called my flop continuation bet, which suggests she has a piece of the board (like a 7 or a small pair) or a draw. By checking back the turn, I control the size of the pot and realize my equity. I still have two overcards to the 7, 5, and 2, and checking allows me to potentially see a cheap showdown or hit a pair on the river. It also protects me from a check-raise on a card that may have hit her range.

I missed completely — K♦8♥ has no pair, no meaningful draws on 2♦-7♠-5♣-J♥-T♦. Flash c‑bet the flop then checked the turn, which looks like pot control or a medium-strength hand; the river Ten could have improved their range (T/J/7/5 combos) but also allows them to check back. With virtually no showdown value and very little fold equity, a bluff here is thin and could be easily called or raised by hands that beat me. I’ll preserve my stack and keep the pot small: check and fold to any sizeable river bet (only consider a call vs a tiny bet if I put them on a high bluff).

I have Queen-high (Qs 9s) on a board of 2d 7s 5c Jh Td. I was the pre-flop aggressor and C-bet the flop, which Minni called. The turn and river both brought overcards (J and T). Minni has checked to me on all three streets. While a bluff might occasionally work here given the scare cards, Minni's check-call on the dry flop suggests she has a piece of the board (like a 7 or 5) or a medium pair. My Queen-high has very little showdown value, but checking back is the most prudent play to conserve my chip lead, as a bluff into a player who has already committed chips on the flop is risky and might be called by any pair or even a strong Ace-high.

Preflop on the button in 3-handed play with Ts7c offsuit. This is a standard stealing spot from the button with position on both blinds. While Ts7o is marginal, in 3-handed the button opening range is wide (around 45-55% of hands), including many offsuit broadway and mid connectors like this. T7o has decent playability postflop IP, with straight potential and a ten blocker. Pot is 150, raising to 300 (3x BB) gives good fold equity against likely wide defending ranges from blinds based on history (Minni and Flash defend but fold often to button opens). Deep stacks but position allows realization. Limping is suboptimal as it bloats pot OOP for blinds. Folding too tight here misses EV from steals over 150 hands.

Fold. I’m in the small blind with K♠6♥ facing a 3bb button open and the big blind still to act. K6o is easily dominated by the button’s opening range and has very poor post‑flop playability out of position; calling 250 to play OOP (and likely multiway) is a negative‑EV spot. A light 3‑bet here would be thin and risky without stronger holdings. Preserve my ~81bb stack and wait for a better, positionally advantaged spot.

I am in the Big Blind with 4c 5s. Elon raised to 300 from the Button and Minni folded. While the pot odds are roughly 2.25:1, 4-5 offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position against a 3x open. Given the tournament stage and my chip lead, there is no reason to defend with low-equity trash hands that lack playability. Folding is the most prudent play here.

I’m on the button with K♦2♠ — a weak but playable hand in position against the blinds. With only the big blind left to act and deep stacks, I want to use my positional advantage and table image to steal the blinds and take the initiative postflop. A standard 3x BB open to 300 is my play: it applies pressure, keeps my opening range wide, and gives me room to fold to a large 3‑bet. Plan: fold K2o to a sizable 3‑bet, otherwise play postflop aggressively (c‑bet favorable boards, control pot on bad boards).

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Trey offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Minni raised from the button, and even though her opening range might be wide, J3o does not play well out of position. Given the stack depths and the hand strength, folding is the most prudent move to preserve my lead.

Q9o is a playable hand in the big blind facing a button min-raise from Minni, who has shown a wide raising range throughout the history (frequently opening to 300 from various positions). The pot odds are favorable: calling an additional 200 into a 450 pot gives about 2.25:1, and post-flop, I have position on Minni after the SB folds, with decent equity (around 40-45% vs a wide button range) and playability for floats, draws, or hitting top pair. Stacks are deep (117 BB effective), so realizing equity is fine. Folding would be too tight; 3-betting risks getting shoved or called wide, while calling balances my range and exploits Minni's aggression.

Top two pair (queens and nines) on a Qc9h6s board is an extremely strong hand, crushing most of Minni's button opening range which is wide based on history (frequent 300 raises). The board has straight draw potential (e.g., T8, J8, 87 suited or not), but no flush draws developed. Acting first heads-up deep-stacked (Minni 78BB eff.), I should bet for thin value against weaker pairs (Qx, 9x), overcards, and draws while protecting against semi-bluffs. Checking risks free cards to draws or check-back of weak hands. Betting ~60% pot (400 into 650) balances value and pot-building without overexposing to raises from her position. Her history shows aggression postflop, so expect calls from worse.

I opened to 300 on the button with K♦2♠ and checked the flop (Qc 9h 6s). Elon (BB) leads for a small 400 into a 1,050 pot. I have no pair and no real draw — only ~6 clean outs (three kings and three deuces), so my raw equity is marginal but the price to continue is attractive. Calling 400 into the ~1,450 pot gives me about 21–22% required equity, and being last to act keeps lots of post‑turn options (realize equity, control pot, or bluff-shove on later streets if the texture and reads allow).

Raising as a pure bluff to 800 is possible, but the raise size is small relative to stacks and Elon has shown willingness to call/continue; I’d be turning a marginal hand into a bloated confrontation without strong fold equity or protection. Against a wide bet/c-bet range, a call in position is the most +EV line: it uses my positional advantage, keeps weaker hands and bluffs in, and allows me to fold to significant aggression on the turn if nothing improves.

Plan: call 400 now. If I pair a K or 2 on the turn I will press for value; if the turn is blank and Elon barrels, I will give up unless the price and read justify continuing. Preserve stack and keep options open rather than forcing a marginal bluff now.

I hold top two pair (queens and nines) on a Qc 9h 6s Qh board, an extremely strong hand against Minni's wide button opening and calling range from history (frequent small raises and calls postflop). The board is relatively dry pre-turn; the Qh pairs the top card without heavily completing draws, though it adds some heart flush draw potential (two hearts now), but her range isn't flush-heavy. No completed straights, limited OESD on flop. Deep stacks (74BB effective), pot 1450, first to act. Betting extracts value from weaker pairs (Qx, 9x), Ax Kx overpairs, missed draws, and floats. Checking risks giving free cards or check-raises. Size ~2/3 pot (1000) builds pot while allowing calls/shoves from worse; if raised, her range is often bluff-heavy or capped, evaluate equity.

Turn is a blank for me (Qc 9h 6s Qh). Elon led flop and fired bigger on the turn (400 -> 1000), a line that strongly indicates Qx/trips or at least a very strong value range. Pot is 2450 and it costs 1000 to call — I’d need roughly 29% equity (1000 / (2450+1000)) to justify a call. I only have ~6 outs (three kings + three deuces) ≈ 13% to improve on the river, and even if I hit I can still be behind (Elon can have Qx or make a full house with KQ on a king river). A raise as a bluff is too risky here versus an opponent who has shown willingness to continue; preserving my ~7470 stack is preferable. Fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with A7o in a 3-handed game. This is a standard opening hand. My stack size is healthy, and I want to put pressure on the blinds. Opening for 3x the big blind is a standard play here.

T4o is a marginal offsuit hand with poor playability, low card strength, and significant gaps. Facing a button raise from Flash, who has been aggressive in prior hands, his range is wide but still favors playable holdings. I'm out of position in the SB, with Minni yet to act in BB. Pot odds are not favorable enough (calling 250 into 450 pot for ~22.7% equity needed, but equity realization OOP with this hand is insufficient against likely calling range or further action). Best to fold and preserve stack for better spots.

Fold. 8d3c is a junk hand — offsuit, disconnected and heavily dominated by a button raising range. I’d be out of position postflop and would have to invest 200 more (to call) for pot odds requiring ~31% equity vs a wide but still superior opening range. A 3-bet bluff is possible but unnecessarily high-variance with deep stacks and a straightforward opponent; preserve 7,370 chips and wait for a better spot or positional advantage.

Preflop on the button facing only the blinds (pot 150, to call 100). 7h9d is a playable hand in late position with straight potential and good postflop playability. With deep stacks and position, raising to steal the blinds or build the pot is standard. Button stealing range includes 79o vs blinds. Common raise size from history is 300. Avoid limping as it's too passive; folding is too tight.

Stack ~7,320 (~73 BB). Facing Elon's open to 300 from the button; pot is 450 and I must add 250 (300 total) to see the flop. I have Q♠T♥ — a marginal but playable broadway hand in a 3-handed game. Elon’s open from the button will be relatively wide, so QTo has decent equity and playability (pairs, straight possibilities, backdoor heart). A min-3-bet to 500 here gives little fold equity, often provokes a call from the big blind, and leaves me out of position; QTo is too weak to 3-bet for value or to reliably fold out better hands. Folding is too nitty given stack depth and table dynamics. Calling keeps the pot controlled, gets to see a flop with reasonable equity, and lets me proceed only when I connect (top pair, good draws) or exploit later streets. Pot odds: I must call 250 into a 450 pot (making it 700) → required equity ≈ 35.7%, which QTo often meets vs a wide open range. Plan: call 300 total; on favorable flops continue for value or with strong draws, otherwise play cautiously out of position and fold to heavy aggression.

I am in the Big Blind with 7s 3d, which is a very weak hand. The action is 300 to me (200 more to call) after an open raise from the Button (Elon) and a call from the Small Blind (Minni). Despite the pot odds, my hand has extremely poor playability post-flop and I am out of position. I have a healthy chip lead and there is no reason to defend with one of the worst possible starting hands in a three-way pot.

I have top pair (Q♠) with a decent T kicker on a dry Q‑8‑3 board. Elon opened wide preflop and checked the flop, so my hand is likely ahead of his checking range (lots of weaker Qx, worse pair+backdoor draws, and some pocket pairs). Betting achieves two goals: extract value from worse queens, tens and some pairs, and deny equity to any backdoor straight/club/spade draws. Pot is 700 — a ~1/2 pot bet keeps weak hands and draws paying to see a turn while not overcommitting out of position. Plan: bet 350; if called reevaluate on turn (C‑bet again on safe turns or check/call if he shows strength); fold to a large check‑raise or apply pot control if the turn becomes dangerous.

Preflop raise with 79o from button was standard for position and steal potential. Flop 8c-Qh-3s is completely dry rainbow board with no connection to my hand: no pair, no straight draw (gutshot or otherwise), no flush draw, pure 9-high air. Minni's SB call preflop followed by a ~1/2 pot bet first in (350 into ~750) represents a strong range hitting top pair (Q), overpairs, or some bluffs/draws, but my hand has zero equity or fold equity against it. Calling commits chips to a multi-street bluff catcher with no position advantage post-turn (wait, actually I have position as button), but even with position, 9-high rarely wins at showdown and pot odds (350 to win 1400, ~20% needed) don't justify as villain likely continues betting better hands. Raising as a bluff is too thin on this dry board without blockers to QQ/88/33/AQ etc., and risks her shoving her 6720 stack. Deep stacks but folding trash preserves stack for better spots. Standard fold.

9♠4♥ is a very weak, unplayable hand even from the button. Although I could attempt a tiny steal, both blinds are deep-stacked and capable of defending or 3-betting — Flash (SB) has shown willingness to play and call, and Elon (BB) is a capable preflop aggressor. Calling 100 to see a flop is cheap, but this hand has poor postflop playability (bad blockers, offsuit, disconnected) and will lose lots of value when called or raised. With ~77 BB effective and lots of hands left in the tournament, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is the right long-term play. Fold and wait for a stronger opening range on the button.

I have Ace-Deuce offsuit in the Small Blind. After the Button folds, it is a blind-versus-blind situation against Elon. Ace-high is a strong starting hand in this scenario. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure on the Big Blind and try to win the pot immediately or play with the range advantage.

Preflop in BB facing a 3x raise from SB after BTN fold. A6o is a marginal but defensible hand against a wide SB opening range, providing good pot odds (calling 200 into 400, roughly 33% needed, and equity around 40-45% vs typical SB range). Ax blocker helps, and deep stacks allow postflop play. Flash has been aggressive but often check-folds or gets outmaneuvered. Will call and play OOP cautiously: check-raise strong hands, check-fold weak if cbet, or check-call floats with ace high. Folding too tight here spews equity.

I have flopped two pair (Aces and Deuces) on a board of Ace, Jack, and Deuce. This is a very strong hand in a blind-versus-blind encounter. While there is a flush draw present, my hand is likely the best at this moment. I will lead with a bet to extract value from weaker Aces, Jacks, or draws that Elon might be holding in the Big Blind.

Flop gives me top pair (aces) with a weak 6 kicker on a draw-heavy board (Jd Ac 2c) with flush draw and straight draw potential. Flash's SB raise preflop and 40% pot cbet is consistent with his aggressive history (often continuation bets wide but folds to resistance postflop, as seen in multiple hands like #6, #15, #35). My Ad blocks strong Ax hands. OOP, but raising builds pot for value/protection against draws (clubs, broadways) in his wide range while denying equity to floats/Jx/weak pairs. Equity vs. his range ~55-65% (blocks nuts, dominates many Ax/Jx). Deep stacks allow; call risks multi-street pressure with weak kicker. Raise to ~3.5x for fold equity and initiative. Minni out, heads-up dynamic favors aggression.

In a blind-versus-blind scenario, bottom two pair (Aces and Deuces) is an exceptionally strong hand. The board contains both flush draws (clubs) and straight draws (KQ), so I want to protect my hand and extract value immediately. Elon's raise to 1400 could signify a strong Ace (AK, AQ), a draw, or a better made hand like AJ or JJ. However, given the ranges in a BvB fight, I cannot simply call and allow draws to realize their equity cheaply. I will reraise to 3000 to put the pressure back on Elon and build the pot while my hand is likely the best.

Facing a large flop 3-bet from aggressive SB Flash after I raised his continuation bet. My top pair weak kicker (Ad6h) blocks some Ax but is dominated by AJ+, AK, AQ, and vulnerable to sets (JJ, AA unlikely), two-pair combos, or strong clubs/Jd. His wide SB opening but strong 3-bet sizing (~2x my raise into ~3k pot pre-3bet) polarizes to nuts/double nuts/draws, reducing my equity to ~30-40% OOP. Draw-heavy board (clubs flush draw, Jd backdoors) worsens implied odds with 6 kicker fearing turn villainization. Deep stacks but calling 1600 (3:1 direct, poor implied) risks multiway pressure later. Previous aggression met resistance; folding high-equity blocker hand preserves ~10k stack for 98 hands left vs history of Flash folding to heat but showing strength here. Better spots abound.

Holding Q7o on the button in a 3-handed game, I am in a strong position to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. This hand is well within the standard opening range for a three-handed button. I will raise to 300 to maintain aggression and leverage my chip lead against the smaller stacks in the blinds.

Preflop from the small blind facing a button raise to 300 (250 to call into 450 pot). My hand 2h Ts is offsuit, disconnected, and low-ranked with poor playability postflop. Flash's button range is wide but still crushes this hand, and Minni (BB) acts behind. Equity is too low even with position considerations after calling; folding preserves stack for better spots in this deep-stacked tournament (hand 53/150). Standard fold to avoid spew.

Facing a 300 open from the button with the SB folded, I'm in the BB with 2♣8♠. To call is 200 into a 450 pot (need ~30.8% equity). 2-8o is a heavily dominated, unplayable hand out of position with poor multi-street equity and realization against a button opening range. A light 3-bet bluff here is too thin versus a deep, aggressive opener who can call or 4-bet; calling leads to difficult postflop decisions OOP. I’ll preserve my stack and fold to the open.

3-handed on the button with Q3o. Excellent position to steal the blinds preflop. Q3o blocks Qx hands and has some high-card value. Opponents have shown aggression historically but are out of position if they defend. Standard open-raise size of 3x BB to build pot and take initiative. My stack ~100BB deep, theirs deeper, so plenty of room postflop. Folding weak hand unopened pot from BTN is too tight; raising has high fold equity (~50-60% vs blinds) and plays well in position if called.

I have pocket fives in the small blind facing a button open to 300. Pot is 450 and it costs me 250 to call (total commitment 300). With ~76BB effective I prefer set-mining here: calling is a small investment for large implied odds if I hit a set, while a 3-bet OOP would bloat the pot and commit more chips with a hand that fares poorly multiway. A min-raise to 500 is too small to get folds and a larger 3-bet just to isolate is unnecessary; calling keeps the pot controlled and preserves stack for post-flop play. Call 300.

I am in the Big Blind with a premium hand, Ace-King offsuit. Elon raised from the button and Minni called from the small blind. This is a classic spot to 3-bet for value and to take control of the hand. Raising to 1300 (roughly 4x the original raise plus the caller) is a standard sizing that puts pressure on both opponents and builds the pot while I have a significant equity advantage.

Facing a 3-bet to 1300 (about 4x my open) from Flash in the BB after Minni's SB call. Q3o is a speculative hand at best for the steal, but it has very little equity against a 3-bet range, which includes pairs, broadways, suited aces, and blockers. Even if Flash is wide (as history shows aggression like in hands 2, 27, 35, 40, 47, 52), continuing OOP for Minni or multiway postflop is disastrous with queen-high offsuit and poor playability. Pot odds are ~2:1 (1000 to call into 2900), requiring ~25% equity, but Q3o has closer to 15-25% vs typical 3-bet range. Minni may call or shove her ~70BB, complicating further. Better to fold and wait for stronger hands/IP advantage in 3-handed play. Stack preservation key in tournament to maximize edge over 150 hands.

I have 5♦5♠ in the small blind facing Flash’s 3‑bet to 1,300 after I cold‑called the button’s 300. Pot is 1,900 and it costs me 1,000 more (total commitment 1,300) with ~73BB effective (stack 7,370). With a small pocket pair and deep stacks the correct plan is set‑mining — call and realize implied odds if I hit a set. The chance to flop a set is ~11.8%; immediate pot odds alone don’t justify a call, but Flash’s 3‑bet range is likely fairly wide and his stack is large, so when I hit I can extract significant value. Folding gives up positive EV; 4‑bet/shove would bloat the pot and commit too many chips with a hand that wants to see a (relatively) cheap flop. I’ll call to continue and plan to play cautiously OOP if I miss (check/fold to heavy aggression or float selectively), and extract max value if I flop a set.

I have pocket 5s on a 9♥ K♠ 6♠ flop and I missed my set. This is a dry board with two overcards to my pair and I’m out of position vs Flash, who 3‑betted preflop and likely has a lot of overcards/strong broadway hands. With ~63BB effective and a pot of 2,900, I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Checking controls the pot, keeps his bluffs in the hand, and preserves stack to leverage my set if I hit. Plan: check and fold to heavy aggression (e.g. a large c‑bet ≳1/2 pot ≈1,450), call small-to-medium c‑bets (≈≤1/3 pot) to see a turn and re-evaluate, and if I hit a set on turn/river extract value (bet/raise/shove).

On a board of Ks-9h-6s, I have Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) with Ace-King. Minni checked to me after calling my pre-flop 3-bet. I need to bet for value against weaker Kings, middle pairs (like TT or JJ), and to charge potential draws, especially the spade flush draw. A bet of approximately 1/3 to 1/2 of the pot is standard in 3-bet pots to maximize value while maintaining a balanced range.

Call. I’m OOP with pocket 5s and missed the 9♥ K♠ 6♠ flop, but Flash 3‑bet preflop and now c‑bet a very small 1,000 into a 3,900 pot (~25% pot). I’ve already committed 300 preflop, so calling the extra 1,000 (total commitment 1,300) is cheap set‑mining and preserves stack for a big score if I hit. Raising would bloat the pot with a weak hand and isolate me against a deeper stack; folding here surrenders positive EV because Flash’s line can include many overcards/bluffs. Plan: call to see the turn and re‑evaluate — if I hit a set I’ll extract value aggressively; if I miss and face heavy turn aggression (large bet/raise, ≳50% pot) I’ll fold, but I’ll continue against small turn bets to realize equity or induce further bluffs.

I called the flop as a set-mining call; I’ve missed the turn (5♦5♠ on 9♥ K♠ 6♠ 3♥) and Flash (the preflop 3‑better) checked to me. I’m out of position with a small pair and limited showdown value against his range, and I don’t want to bloat a pot of 4,900 with only a vulnerable pair and ~5,370 behind. Checking controls the pot, lets me realize equity and keeps bluffs in, and preserves my stack for a legitimate set if I hit the river. If he barrels the river big I’ll fold; I’ll only call small river bets or extract value aggressively if I actually hit. Action: check.

I have Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) with Ace-King on a board of Ks-9h-6s-3h. The turn 3h is a brick that doesn't significantly change the board state. Given the current pot of 4900 and Minni's remaining stack of 5370, the Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) is approximately 1.1, making me pot-committed with this strength of hand. I will bet 2200 to extract value from weaker Kings like KQ or KJ and to charge any spade flush draws. This sizing sets up a potential river shove while maintaining pressure on her range.

I’m holding 5♦5♠ and have missed the 9♥ K♠ 6♠ 3♥ board. Flash 3‑bet preflop and has now bet 2,200 into a 7,100 pot (~31% pot). Raising or shoving OOP with a low pair would needlessly bloat the pot with a hand that only has two river outs to a set (~4.3%), so that’s the wrong line. Folding would surrender potential positive EV vs his wide c‑bet/barrel frequency — his line (preflop 3‑bet, very small flop c‑bet, small turn bet) contains many overcards and bluffs that I can call down cheaply. Calling 2,200 here preserves my stack, realizes equity, keeps bluffs in the pot, and keeps the option to fold to big river aggression if I miss. Plan: call to see the river; if I hit a set I’ll extract value aggressively, otherwise re‑evaluate on the river and fold to large bets.

I have 5♦5♠ on 9♥ K♠ 6♠ 3♥ Q♥ — bottom pair with no heart blocker and virtually no showdown value vs a preflop 3‑better. Pot is 9,300 and my stack is 3,170. Flash (the 3‑better) has been betting small lines on flop/turn and now checked the river, which usually indicates pot‑control with Kx/Qx or a missed hand looking to induce a bluff. Betting here would be thin value (called by hands that beat me, folded by worse infrequently) and risks committing a large portion of my stack to a raise. With no clean bluffs to target and low equity, the prudent, +EV line is to check back and see a showdown rather than bloating the pot OOP.

I have Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) with Ace-King. The board is 9h-Ks-6s-3h-Qh. Minni has checked to me on the river. The pot is 9300 and Minni only has 3170 remaining. Given the low Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) and the strength of my hand, this is a clear value-betting situation. While the Queen on the river could potentially have improved a hand like KQ, Minni's range contains many more King-x hands that I dominate (such as KJ or KT) which she is likely to call with given the pot odds. Since she has so little behind, I will move her all-in for her remaining 3170 to maximize my value.

This is a river showdown decision: I have 5♦5♠ on 9♥ K♠ 6♠ 3♥ Q♥. Pot = 12,470 and it costs me my last 3,170 to call (I’d need ~20.3% equity to make a call break-even). Flash 3‑bet preflop and has fired small, persistent bets (small flop c‑bet, small turn barrel, now a relatively small river wager). That line is textbook for extracting thin value from Kx/Qx/overpairs and for getting called by worse; the river Q and the third heart also give him many clear value/shove/flush combos that beat my small pair. My 5s have almost no showdown value here (I don’t block Q/heart combos and only beat rare pure bluffs or ridiculously light holdings). Against his range my realistic equity is well below the ~20% required. Committing my last chips to a likely value-heavy range is unnecessary and I should preserve chips for better spots. Fold.

I'm on the button with K♣?K♥? (Kh Js) and ~31.7 BB — a clear open-raise spot. Raising isolates the blinds and leverages position and fold equity; I don't want to give the blinds a cheap price to see a flop. A standard 3x BB open (300 total) is appropriate: large enough to discourage limp-calling but not so large that I overcommit. Plan: fold to a very large 3-bet, consider calling a small 3-bet depending on sizing/reads; if called, play aggressively postflop with c-bets on favorable boards and proceed cautiously on heavy Ace-high textures.

I am in the Small Blind with 4s 3h, an extremely weak hand. Minni, who is short-stacked with under 30 BBs, has raised to 300 from the button. Given the weakness of my hand and the fact that I would be out of position for the rest of the hand against a player who is likely looking to commit their remaining chips soon, there is no reason to defend this hand. Folding is the most prudent play to maintain my chip lead.

2s6d is a complete trash hand offsuit with poor playability, low cards, and gaps. Facing a button raise from Minni, who opens frequently from late position. Pot odds are 450:200 (2.25:1, ~31% equity needed), but this hand has low equity (~30% or less) vs. a wide BTN range and poor postflop playability OOP. Standard BB defense folds this. Preserve stack for better spots; no need to defend this wide.

I have Ace-Seven offsuit on the button, which is a strong opening hand in 3-handed play. With both opponents having smaller stacks, especially Minni who is quite short, I want to apply pressure and either win the blinds immediately or play a pot in position with the best hand. A standard 3x raise is consistent with my previous betting patterns.

Pocket 7s (77) is a premium hand for 3-betting from the small blind against a button raise. Flash's button opening range is likely wide based on history of frequent raises. 77 performs well against that range (~82% equity vs random, still strong vs polarized raise). 3-betting builds the pot, gets value from worse hands, isolates if Minni folds, and denies equity to calling hands. Folding is too tight preflop. Calling OOP risks difficult postflop spots, especially with Minni behind. Standard sizing is 3x the open (900 total bet). Minni's short stack (32BB) adds fold equity. This maximizes EV long-term.

I’m in the big blind with Qh6h facing a button open to 300 and a small-blind 3-bet to 900. To call is 800 more (about 8bb, ~25% of my 32bb stack). If I call the pot will be ~2100 and I’ll have ~2320 behind (SPR ≈ 1.1) — essentially committing myself to big decisions postflop with a weak, often-dominated hand. The immediate pot odds on a call are roughly 800 / 2100 ≈ 38%, and Q6s rarely has that much equity versus a 3-bettor’s range (and this will likely be multiway). A raise to 1500 or shoving is too aggressive with such a marginal holding and invites stronger action from both players. With a healthy ~32bb stack I should preserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold preflop.

I have Ace-Seven offsuit on the button and opened for 300. Elon 3-bet from the small blind to 900. While A7o isn't a premium hand, I have a massive chip lead and position on Elon for the rest of the hand. Folding is too conservative here, and calling allows me to see a flop and use my positional advantage to evaluate the strength of my hand. I am calling the additional 600 to see the flop.

Flop is Kc8d4s, rainbow and dry with no obvious draws. My pocket 77 is an overpair to the 8 and 4, giving me strong equity against Flash's 3-bet calling range from the button, which likely includes broadway hands like AKo/AKQ, KQ, some suited connectors, and pocket pairs below KK. The king hits top of his range, but 77 has ~40-50% equity vs a typical polarized calling range (e.g., beats Ax, pairs below K, draws; loses to sets/Kx). As the preflop 3-bettor OOP, I should c-bet for value from worse (overcards, lower pairs), protection against Ax improving, and to deny equity to floats/draws. Checking risks giving free cards to overcards or inducing bluffs later. Flash is aggressive and deep-stacked; a bet sizes him up and builds pot when ahead. Pot 1900, betting ~2/3 pot (1200) is standard sizing: small enough for fold equity vs air, large enough for value. No reason to check-raise on this dry board.

I called Elon's 3-bet pre-flop to play the hand in position, but the flop of King-Eight-Four is very favorable for a 3-bettor's range, which includes hands like AK, KQ, and strong pocket pairs. Elon's continuation bet of 1200 into a 1900 pot shows significant strength. With only Ace-high and no draws on this dry board, my equity is low against his likely range. I will fold here to preserve my stack and avoid a potentially expensive mistake against a committed opponent.

Preflop on the button in a 3-handed pot with blinds posted (pot 150, to call 100). Hand is Qs3c offsuit, queen-high with a terrible kicker and no playability (not suited, not connected). Even 3-handed from the button, Q3o is outside my opening range as it's easily dominated by better queens, has poor equity when called, and minimal bluff value postflop. Opponents: Minni short-stacked at ~32BB (aggressive raiser historically but folds to pressure), Flash deep. Better to fold and wait for a stronger spot to steal blinds or play in position with equity. Preserves stack for value spots.

I have K♣ J♠ in the small blind with two players (button folded), ~31 BB effective. This is a strong heads-up hand and I want to seize the initiative and apply pressure to the big blind. A 3x-ish open to 300 (total) gives good fold equity, narrows Flash’s defending range, and keeps the pot manageable with my stack size. Plan: raise to 300, c-bet favorable flops and re-evaluate if Flash 3-bets (fold to very large 3-bets that commit a big portion of my stack).

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Trey suited. The Small Blind, Minni, has raised to 300. With a stack of 25,620, calling 200 more to see a flop heads-up against a short-stacked opponent (Minni has under 30 BBs) is a standard play given the pot odds and the fact that I will have position post-flop.

Flop: Qc Qd 8h. I opened to 300 from the SB and Flash called. I hold Kc Js — no pair, no meaningful draw. This flop heavily favors the caller’s range (lots of Qx, some 8x, and many sticky broadways). Flash has shown a tendency to call down and defend wide, so a c‑bet here will be called too often by hands that already beat me. Pot is 600 and my stack is ~29BB; I don’t want to bloat a pot OOP with a marginal hand. Plan: check and control the pot. If Flash fires a small probe (≈1/3 pot ≲200) I can call to probe further; I’ll fold to a bigger, committed bet (≈1/2 pot or larger ~300+), and if he checks back I’ll re‑evaluate the turn for showdown value or any improvement before committing chips.

I have flopped trips with a Queen in my hand and two on the board. The board is very dry (8h-Qc-Qd), meaning there are few draws for my opponent to have. Since Minni, the pre-flop raiser, checked to me, she likely has air or a weak showdown hand like a pocket pair or an 8. By checking back, I disguise the strength of my hand, avoid forcing her to fold her bluffs, and allow her the opportunity to either bluff the turn or improve to a second-best hand that can provide value on later streets. Given her short stack, there is plenty of time to get the chips in if she finds a reason to call.

Turn brought J♦ giving me two pair (Queens and Jacks) with K♣ J♠. Flop was checked by both and Flash has shown a wide, sticky calling range — so his range contains a lot of worse hands (Kx, Jx, weak pairs, some draws) but also some Qx hands that currently beat me. This is a clear spot to bet for value and to charge any draws while denying a free river that could give a queen/full house to a Qx hand. I’ll size ~2/3 pot to get called by worse but not overcommit: bet 400 into the 600 pot. If Flash check‑raises large I’ll heavily suspect trips/full house and will re‑evaluate/fold to a committed raise; if he calls I’ll control the river and extract/defend accordingly.

I have trips (Queens) with a weak kicker (Trey). Minni checked the flop and is now leading for 400 on the turn Jack of Diamonds. With her short stack (about 25 big blinds remaining), I want to keep her range as wide as possible. If I raise now, I likely only get called by better Queens (AQ, KQ, QJ, QT) or hands that have me beat like pocket Jacks or Eights. By calling, I allow her to continue bluffing with air or value betting a Jack or an Eight on the river, where I can then decide whether to raise or just call down based on the final board texture.

I have K♣ J♠ for two pair (Queens and Jacks) on Qc Qd Jd 8h 2h. Flash’s line (wide defending range, checked the flop and just called my turn bet) contains a meaningful number of Qx and some J/Q full-house combos that beat me, plus worse hands (Kx, single Jx, weaker pairs) that I beat. Betting the river would mainly charge hands that already beat me and risk getting called or even check‑raised by the hands that dominate me; it would only extract thin value from a few Kx combos. With a 1,400 pot and 2,520 stack and given Flash’s sticky call tendencies, the lowest-variance, +EV play is to check back and take showdown. If he leads out small I can call; a large raise would strongly indicate trips/full house and I would fold.

On a board of 8h-Qc-Qd-Jd-2h, I hold trip Queens with a weak kicker. Minni (SB) checked the flop, bet the turn, and has now checked the river. She is playing a short stack (approx. 25 BBs). Her turn lead followed by a river check suggests a marginal hand like a Jack, an Eight, or a mid-pocket pair that she is now trying to take to showdown cheaply. While there are several better Queens (AQ, KQ, QJ, QT, Q9) and some full houses (88, JJ, QJ) in her range, the fact that she checked the river suggests she is likely not holding a monster. I will place a small value bet of 600, which is less than half the pot, to entice a call from her marginal made hands like Jx or 8x.

I have K♣ J♠ for two pair (Qs and Js) on Qc Qd Jd 8h 2h. Pot is 2000 and Flash has just bet 600; calling 600 gives ~23% pot‑odds (600/(2000+600)). Flash called my sizable turn bet after a checked flop, which is consistent with a wide, sticky calling range that contains many worse hands (Kx, single Jx with worse kicker, medium pairs, missed draws) as well as some hands that beat me (any Q, pocket pairs that make full houses/quads). His river sizing is fairly small — both a thin value/blocking bet and a plausible bluff/feeler. Raising would risk committing extra chips to a range that includes the hands that crush me and would fold out many of the hands I want value from. Given the pot odds, his line, and my stack depth, the lowest‑variance +EV play is to call and go to showdown. If he were to jam or make a large raise, I would strongly suspect trips/full house and fold.

I’m on the button with Jc7c and about 19.2 BB (stack 1920). Pot is 150 and it’s folded to me — J7s is playable in position, has decent postflop playability (backdoor flushs/top-pair potential) and good fold equity as an opener. With three-handed and shortish stacks I want to take the initiative and steal the blinds while keeping postflop maneuvering room. I’ll make a standard button open (size between 2–3x the BB) to apply pressure but not commit myself. If I get isolated or called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable runouts and look for clubs/Jack connectivity; if I face a large 3-bet shove I’ll fold this marginal hand. Raising to 300 total balances fold equity and postflop playability.

Minni is the short stack with only 1,620 chips (approx. 16 BB). Her 3x open to 300 indicates she is likely committed to the hand. While Q9o has some equity, playing it out of position against a short stack who is looking for a double-up is risky, especially with Elon (a competent stack) still to act in the Big Blind. Folding keeps my chip lead secure and allows me to find better spots to pressure the smaller stacks.

Preflop in the big blind facing a button raise to 300 (200 to call into 450 pot). My hand 7s5d is offsuit, low, disconnected, and has poor playability out of position. Minni's frequent raising from button suggests a wide range, but this hand has negligible equity against it and isn't worth defending for thin value or implied odds given stacks and my position. Standard fold to avoid difficult postflop spots.

I am on the button with Ac4c, which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. My stack is very healthy (269.7 BB), while Minni is quite short-stacked with only 19.7 BB. I will put pressure on the blinds by raising to my standard sizing of 300. If Minni shoves, I have sufficient equity and pot odds to call her off, given her short stack.

Facing a raise to 300 from Flash on the button, who has been aggressive in prior hands with frequent preflop raises and continuation bets. My hand 3h5c is extremely weak: offsuit, low, poorly connected, with minimal playability postflop, especially out of position in the small blind. Pot odds are about 1.8:1 (250 to call into 700), but equity against a wide button range is poor (~25-30% at best). Minni is short-stacked behind and may shove, complicating further. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots in this multi-hand tournament.

Flash opened to 300 from the button; I’m in the big blind with Qh9s and a 1,970 stack (~19.7 BB). The pot is 450 and it costs me 200 more to call (pot will be ~650), so my break-even equity is only ~31%. Q9o is a playable hand against a wide button opening range — it has reasonable showdown and straight/second-pair potential. With only ~20 BB effective, an aggressive 3-bet or shove is an option, but 3-betting out of position with a marginal hand risks bloating the pot and getting called by better hands; shoving would be too polar and often dominated. I prefer to call the 200 to see a flop, keep pots manageable, realize equity, and retain the option to fold to heavy action on unfavorable textures. If Flash shows significant strength (large 3-bet/ shove) I will fold post-flop or to further preflop aggression.

Check. On A♦6♠7♥ my Q♥9♠ has no pair and no meaningful draw — it’s currently behind to any A‑x and many paired hands from a button open. With a short stack (1,770 ≈ 17.7BB) I want pot control out of position and don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. By checking I force Flash to declare: if he C‑bets small (roughly ≤ ~300) I’ll call and try to realize equity to the turn; if he barrels large or raises (≈ >300–400) I’ll fold and preserve my stack. If he checks back we keep the pot small and can try to improve cheaply. This is the lowest-variance, highest-EV line here.

I have top pair with an Ace on a very dry board (Ad-6s-7h). My opponent, Minni, is quite short-stacked with only about 1770 chips remaining. I will lead with a continuation bet of approximately 40% of the pot to extract value from weaker hands like mid-pairs or straight draws (89, 58) and to protect my hand. Given her stack size, if she raises, I am likely committed to calling or shoving.

Flash opened from the button and I defended the big blind with Q♥9♠. On A♦6♠7♥ he leads for 250 into a 900 pot — calling 250 gives me ~21.7% pot odds. Q9o has roughly the required equity to improve to a pair by the river (plus tiny backdoor straight chance) and Flash’s small probe-bet is consistent with a wide c-bet range. With ~17.7 BB effective I want to keep the pot controllable and realize equity rather than bloating the pot with a marginal hand, so I’ll call the small bet and re-evaluate on the turn. I will fold to significant turn aggression or raise only with a clear plan/strong draw.

Board: Ad 6s 7h Td. My hand Qh9s is currently unpaired with only a 4‑out straight draw (any 8) — ~8.7% to hit the river. Pot is 1,150 and my stack is 1,520 (~15.2 BB); Flash is very deep. Flash’s small flop probe and my call are consistent with a wide range that includes many Ax, Tx, small pairs and air. With only one card to come and such thin equity, I should avoid bloating the pot OOP. Checking preserves my stack, keeps the pot controllable, and allows me to see a free river or fold to meaningful aggression. If Flash bets a small amount on the river I can call to realize my 4 outs; I will fold to large river aggression or a shove since my equity is too low. Shoving or a significant bet here would be too polar and will often be called by better hands, so the highest EV line is to check and re-evaluate on the river.

I have top pair on a board that is becoming more coordinated with the Ten of diamonds. Minni has checked twice and is playing a very short stack (around 15 BBs). I want to continue extracting value from her range, which likely includes weaker Aces, middle pairs (7x, 6x, or now 10x), or straight draws like 89. A bet of 500 into a pot of 1150 is a standard value bet that maintains pressure and sets up a potential river shove given her remaining chips.

Pot was 1,150 before the turn; Flash bets 500, making the pot 1,650 and it costs me 500 to call. Calling gives me 500/(1,650+500) ≈ 23.3% break-even equity. My Q♥9♠ on A♦6♠7♥T♦ has only 4 clean outs (any 8) to a straight (~8.7% on the river). There are 3 queens and 3 nines that can pair me, but many of those pairs still lose to Ax or Tx — hands very plausible in Flash’s line (button open, small c-bet, turn continuation). Raw outs (10/46 ≈ 21.7%) are below the pot‑odds threshold and are much less “clean” once you consider opponent ranges. With a short stack (~15 BB) I should avoid committing chips with such thin equity; shoving is too polar and will be called by better hands, and calling is a marginal investment that will likely force difficult decisions on the river. Therefore the +EV play here is to fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

3-handed on the button with J9s suited connectors, a strong speculative hand with great playability, position, and flush/straight potential. Pot is 150 (blinds posted, SB likely folded given no action listed and to-call of 100 matching BB). Facing only BB (Flash, deep-stacked at 274 BB), standard play is to raise for value, isolation, and dead money from blinds. History shows frequent 300 opens preflop by all players. Raise to 3x BB (300 total) balances stealing blinds vs value. Minni's short stack (15 BB) is dead money if folded. Excellent spot to build pot OOP vs BB.

I’m in the small blind with T6 offsuit and only 1,470 chips (~14.7bb) facing a button open to 3bb (300). The pot is 450 and it costs me 250 to call (250/(450+250) ≈ 35.7% break‑even equity). T6o is heavily dominated by a BTN open range (aces, broadways, pairs) and won’t have the required equity often. Shoving all‑in would require ~76% fold equity to be break‑even, which is unrealistic against this lineup; a small reraise to 500 leaves awkward stacks and poor postflop playability. With a short stack and a marginal, dominated hand, the +EV play is to preserve chips and fold here.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Deuce offsuit. Elon raised from the Button. Although the pot odds are roughly 2.25 to 1, this hand is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position. Given my chip lead, there is no reason to defend with a marginal hand and risk bleeding chips against the second-largest stack. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I'm on the button with A♥2♦ and a 1470 stack (~14.7bb). Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to see the flop. Button open-raising is standard here to take advantage of position and fold equity. I prefer a 3bb open to 300 (total) — big enough to deny equity to the blinds and consistent with table tendencies, while leaving ~1170 (≈11.7bb) behind to either shove over a small 3-bet or continue post-flop. Shoving now would be too exploitable given the deep blinds; simply calling is too passive. I raise to 300 to maximize EV while preserving flexibility; I will re-evaluate if I face a 3-bet (shove vs small/short-range 3-bets, fold or call depending on sizing and reads).

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Five offsuit, which is a strong hand in three-handed play. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300, but she has a very short stack of only 1170 chips (approximately 12 big blinds). By raising to 1170, I effectively put her all-in, isolating her and preventing the Big Blind (Elon) from easily entering the pot. Given the pot odds and her likely wide range on the button, A5o is a solid hand to commit with against her remaining stack.

AcJc is a premium suited ace hand with excellent playability, blockers to AA/AKc/AJc, and flush potential. Facing a button open (Minni opens wide) and SB 3bet (Flash aggressive from history), 4betting for value/thin value and initiative OOP is standard. Call would be passive multiway OOP; fold too tight. Sizing ~2.5x the 3bet (1170*2.5=2925, round to 3000) commits ~27% of stack but pot odds improve post-4bet, and stacks deep (109BB). Minni likely folds wide range, Flash can have bluffs to fold. Builds pot with equity advantage long-term.

I opened to 300 on the button with A♥2♦ and I have 1,170 left (~11.7bb). Flash (SB) 3-bet to 1,170 and Elon (BB) 4-bet to 3,000. Calling would commit my entire remaining stack (all‑in for the additional 1,170, total contribution 1,470) and the pot would be ~5,640 — so I need roughly 1,170/5,640 ≈ 20.7% equity to break even. Facing a polarized, very strong 4‑bet range (AA/KK/QQ/AK) and a 3‑bettor still in the hand, A2o is heavily dominated multi‑way and my realistic equity is below the required threshold. I planned to shove only vs small/short‑range 3‑bets, not vs a large 4‑bet from a deep stack. Folding preserves my short stack and lets me wait for a better spot — therefore I fold.

Elon's cold 4-bet from the Big Blind after my 3-bet is an extremely strong signal, typically representing a premium range such as JJ+, AK, or occasionally AQ. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (approximately 29%), Ace-Five offsuit is likely dominated by his calling/raising range. Playing a bloated pot out of position with a marginal hand against the second-largest stack is unnecessarily risky. I will fold and maintain my chip lead.

I am on the button with Ace-Seven offsuit. In a three-handed game, this is a strong starting hand. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300. Minni is extremely short-stacked (roughly 10 BB), so my raise puts significant pressure on her. If she decides to move all-in for her remaining 1,070 chips, I will likely call given the pot odds and the strength of my hand relative to a 10 BB shoving range.

T7o is a marginal offsuit hand with poor connectivity and playability. Facing a button raise from aggressive Flash, my SB defend range shouldn't include this. Pot odds are about 1.8:1, but equity realization postflop is low against a wide button range. Minni is very short-stacked (10BB), but that doesn't improve my hand. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I hold A♣5♣ in the big blind facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, to-call 200). My effective stack is 1,070 (~10.7 BB). With this short stack A5s is in a shove/push range: it has solid showdown equity versus the button’s wide opening range and I get meaningful fold equity as well. Calling 200 would leave me OOP with only ~8.7 BB behind and make post‑flop play difficult; a small raise to 500 leaves too much dead money and still allows the button to see a cheap flop or 3‑bet. Shoving maximizes fold equity, simplifies decisions, and preserves good equity when called. I will shove my stack.

Minni has moved all-in for 1,070 chips (10.7 BB). I have already committed 300, and it costs 770 more to call. The total pot is 1,420. I need approximately 35.2% equity to call profitably. A7o is a strong hand three-handed and is expected to have over 50% equity against a wide 10 BB shoving range from the Big Blind. Mathematically, this is a clear call to potentially eliminate the short stack.

I shoved most of my stack pre‑flop and was called; I’ve got A♣5♣ on a Jh–8s–2d flop — I’ve missed and only have a backdoor club/straight chance and Ace‑high. The pot is ~2190 while I have only 100 left, so betting/shoving 100 into this pot buys almost no fold equity (≈4–5% of the pot) and is unlikely to fold enough of Flash’s calling range (Jx, pairs, broadways). Better to check and let the opponent reveal intent: if he bets a tiny amount I can call with great pot odds, if he barrels large I can fold and preserve my tiny remaining stack. Checking also keeps the decision simple and avoids throwing away chips for negligible upside.

Minni has only 100 chips left in her stack while the pot is already over 2,000. She is effectively all-in. Since she checked, I will bet 100 to put her all-in. I have Ace-high, which is likely the best hand or has significant equity against her range. There is no strategic reason to check back and give a free card when the bet is so small relative to the pot.

Flash checked the pot after my check and then fired a tiny bet of 100 into ~2290. Calling 100 to win a ~2390 pot requires only ~4.2% equity (100 / 2390) — trivially small. I have A♣5♣ on J♥–8♠–2♦: there are no clubs on the board so a flush is impossible, but I have 3 Aces + 3 Fives as clean outs (6 outs), which gives ≈24.1% to hit at least one pair by the river (1 - (41/47)*(40/46)). Runner‑runner straight possibilities add a little more. Even vs a relatively strong calling range from Flash, my raw equity comfortably exceeds the break‑even requirement. Raising/shoving 100 here would buy almost no fold equity and be a thin gamble; folding 100 surrenders a very large pot for scant benefit. With these pot odds and my remaining stack, the correct play is to call my last 100 and take my chances to improve.

3-handed on the button with Q7o, a speculative queen-high hand with decent blockers and postflop playability in position. Blinds posted pot 150, Minni SB short at ~11BB (likely fold or shove), Flash BB deep but button opens steal wide. Standard open to 3x BB (300 total) for fold equity and to win dead money. Limp bad, fold too tight given position.

I’m the small blind with 1,145 chips (~11.45 BB) facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, I must call 250). J♣3♦ is a weak, offsuit hand with poor post‑flop playability — at this stack depth we should be in a push/fold mindset. Calling 250 leaves me shallow and likely to face squeezes or difficult multiway spots with a dominated hand; a min‑raise to 500 still leaves awkward postflop stacks and much less fold equity. Shoving maximizes fold equity against the button’s open and avoids post‑flop decisions; if I get called I accept the variance for a chance to double up. Given tournament dynamics and my short stack, I will shove all‑in.

I am holding K5o in the Big Blind. Elon opened from the Button for 300, and Minni 3-bet all-in for 1145 total. While Minni's range is likely very wide given her extremely short stack (50 chips remaining), K5o is a weak hand to play out of position against Elon, who still has the option to call or re-raise. Calling 10.5 big blinds with a dominated hand in a potentially multi-way pot is not profitable. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

Q7o is a marginal button open that got 3-bet by short-stacked SB Minni for nearly all-in (1145 total, 50 behind). Her sizing is large (~3.8x), indicating strength or desperation with a polarized range heavy on premiums (JJ+, AKo, AQs+). Q7o has a Q blocker but offsuit, poor playability, and ~25-30% equity at best vs that range, insufficient vs pot odds of ~1.8:1 (calling 845 to win 1545, needing ~35%). Raising adds fold equity but risks calling off 50 more postflop with worse; calling risks domination OOP postflop if she has AQ/KQ. History shows Minni aggressive but this spot exploits wide button opens. Standard fold preserves deep stack (12k) for superior edges in 3-handed play over 150-hand tournament.
